game_meta.title: Kailani Rising: A Humanitarian Roleplay
game_meta.subtitle: A comprehensive branching narrative covering all nine Core Humanitarian Standard commitments
game_meta.status: skeleton — all 9 CHS commitments covered; use development_prompts to expand each section
game_meta.target_audience: Humanitarian and development sector workers, trainers, and students
game_meta.chs_reference: Core Humanitarian Standard on Quality and Accountability, 2024
game_meta.description: You are Maya Chen, newly appointed Country Director for HopeForward International in Kailani — a Pacific island nation devastated by Cyclone Vera. Over nine critical decision chapters — one for each CHS commitment — you navigate community rights and participation, timely and effective assistance, building resilience, avoiding harm, complaints and feedback, coordination, learning and improvement, staff management, and ethical resource stewardship. Every choice you make references specific CHS sub-requirements. Choices set context flags that cascade into later decisions, creating real interdependencies. There are four possible endings, shaped by your cumulative score across four dimensions and the flags you have set. All 45 CHS requirements are addressed across the nine decision chapters.
game_meta.how_to_play: Read each scene, then choose one of four responses. Choices set context flags that shape the situation you face in later chapters. At the end, your cumulative scores across four dimensions determine which of four endings you reach. After each choice, read the consequence scene — it shows the immediate AND delayed impact of your decision, and which CHS requirements you met or missed. Look for the 'cascading_note' to see how today's choice changes tomorrow's options.

scoring.scale_note: Scores accumulate across all nine decisions. Each dimension can earn up to +2 per decision (max +18 per dimension). Negative scores are possible for poor choices.
scoring.note: Sum all four dimension scores. With nine decisions, maximum possible total is approximately 56. Thresholds above are minimum scores for each tier.

player_character.name: Maya Chen
player_character.backstory_summary: Eight years of field experience across Asia and East Africa. This is Maya's first Country Director role. She is under pressure from headquarters, trusted by her team, and committed to the communities she serves. She trained in social work and has a master's in humanitarian action. Her closest advisor is Sione, a Kailani national with deep community networks. She believes in principled, accountable, locally-led response — but faces constant pressure to cut corners for speed and cost.
player_character.image_prompt: PLACEHOLDER — Illustration of Maya Chen, Country Director, East Asian woman mid-thirties, short dark hair, HopeForward International light-blue field vest with logo, holding a clipboard, standing at a coastal Pacific island displacement camp. Cyclone damage in background: fallen palm trees, blue tarp shelters. Determined but compassionate expression. Flat digital illustration style, warm Pacific light.

setting.description_summary: A fictional Pacific island nation. Cyclone Vera (Category 4) made landfall 72 hours ago. 85,000 people are displaced. Coastal villages are destroyed, roads are cut, aid is arriving slowly. Government capacity is stretched. Active local civil society but under-resourced. The response unfolds over a six-month period: immediate response (decisions 1-4), stabilisation (decisions 5-7), and transition and exit (decisions 8-9).

context_flags_system.description: Context flags are set by certain choices and shape the situation you face in later decisions. Flags are tracked by the game engine. Each option's 'sets_flag' field shows what it activates. Later decision scenes reference active flags in their situation_summary to reflect the cumulative consequences of your choices. Flags cannot be reversed once set.

scenes.title: Day One — Cyclone Vera's Aftermath
scenes.narrative_summary: Maya's plane descends through torn clouds into Port Hana. Below: blue tarps where roofs once were, brown floodwater threading through streets, aid trucks queuing at a checkpoint. Seventy-two hours since Cyclone Vera. 85,000 displaced. Sione Taufa, HopeForward's National Programme Officer, already has the measure of things. HopeForward's Kailani programme exists on paper only — Maya has three weeks to stand it up. Her first decision begins now.
scenes.image_prompt: Aerial view descending into Port Hana, Kailani, a Pacific island nation. Through torn post-cyclone clouds: a coastline of cyclone-damaged coastal villages — palm trees snapped, fishing boats scattered on roads, clusters of blue tarp shelters gleaming against storm-grey debris. Brown floodwater threading through streets inland. Aid trucks queuing at a military checkpoint on the runway approach road. The capital Port Hana visible in the distance, a UN coordination flag flying at the relief hub. Maya Chen's silhouette faint in the aircraft window — East Asian woman, mid-thirties, dark hair, HopeForward light-blue field vest. Flat digital illustration, storm-clearing Pacific light breaking through grey overcast, teal and charcoal palette with relief-blue accents.
scenes.narrative:
The plane drops through the last of the cloud and Kailani appears — not the Pacific postcard Maya had studied in satellite images, but something torn. Below her, a coastline stripped bare: palms snapped at their roots, fishing boats heaped in impossible places, blue tarps shuddering over the broken geometry of coastal homes. Cyclone Vera made landfall seventy-two hours ago. Eighty-five thousand people are sleeping somewhere tonight that is not their own bed.

Maya's phone buzzes: fourteen unread messages. Out the porthole, she can already see the military checkpoint at the end of Port Hana's runway, two aid trucks queuing behind a barrier of sandbags. Brown floodwater threads through the streets to the east.

A hand rises at the back of the cabin. Sione Taufa, HopeForward's National Programme Officer — twenty-nine years old, born and raised two islands over from here — holds up a printed page covered in handwritten annotations.

"The community consultation schedule," he says, leaning across the aisle. "The NGO coordination meeting clashes with the Tevita village elders. I moved us. The community comes first."

She takes the paper. Outside, the wheels scrape the tarmac. The smell of salt and wet earth fills the cabin as the door is opened.

HopeForward's Kailani programme exists on paper only. She has three weeks to stand it up, a stretched team, a headquarters she cannot fully trust, and communities whose names she cannot yet pronounce. The weight of it settles in her chest, sharp and clarifying.

Her first decision begins now.

scenes.title: Decision 1 — How Will You Hear the Community?

chs_commitment.title: People and communities can exercise their rights and participate in actions and decisions that affect them.

all_requirements.1.1: Ensure diversity, equity and inclusion considerations are integrated into the organisation's work with people and communities, with attention to the most marginalised.
all_requirements.1.2: Regularly share relevant and timely information with people and communities, including about their rights in relation to the commitments and responsibilities of the organisation.
all_requirements.1.3: Communicate in languages and formats that are easily accessible, understandable, respectful and contextually appropriate for people and communities.
all_requirements.1.4: Ensure people's participation in decisions and actions is meaningful for them and corresponds to their preferred ways of engaging.
all_requirements.1.5: Communications representing people and communities, including those used for advocacy and fundraising, have their informed consent, are accurate, respectful, ethical and preserve their dignity.
all_requirements.1.6: Establish a coherent organisational approach to ensure transparent information-sharing, communication and meaningful participation of people and communities in the actions and decisions that affect them.

scenes.situation_summary: Day 2. HopeForward needs an initial needs assessment before programming begins. Sione warns Maya that Manu Camp has three distinct communities: coastal fishing families (Kailani language primary), inland agricultural households (Tevita dialect), and a group of 340 people with disabilities and elderly individuals housed in a separate section with almost no external contact. A government official is pushing for a rapid written survey in English. A local activist is asking why communities haven't been told what HopeForward is or what rights they have. KCV's Filomena has offered to co-facilitate a structured community dialogue — but it will take 3 days instead of 3 hours. HQ wants a preliminary assessment by end of day.
scenes.image_prompt: Manu Displacement Camp, morning, Day 2 after Cyclone Vera. Maya Chen stands in the open camp yard at a folding table covered in printed maps and a government survey form. To her left: Sione Taufa, HopeForward field vest, field notebook open, jaw set with concern. To her right: a sharp-suited government official, pen tapping an English-language questionnaire. Across the table: Filomena Vaka, KCV executive director, floral blouse and lanyard, holding a 3-day facilitation schedule. Behind the four adults, three distinct groupings of displaced people are visible at the far edge of the frame — coastal families near makeshift fishing gear, farming households with bundles of tools, and a quiet corner where elderly and disabled residents sit apart from the main activity under a patched tarpaulin. Tension between the four standing figures. Flat digital illustration, warm Pacific morning light, complex crowd composition, relief-blue and ochre palette.

options.label: Rapid written survey in English via government enumerators
options.description_summary: Accept the government's method. English-language written questionnaire, enumerators cover the main camp in 3 hours.
requirements_missed: 1.1 — misses disability section and dialect speakers
1.3 — wrong language and format for most communities
options.chs_learning_note: Speed without inclusion produces biased data. The marginalised groups are invisible in the results.
options.consequence_summary: Assessment is fast, but women-only spaces, the disability section, and dialect speakers are entirely missed. Programme design is based on the needs of those who were easiest to reach.
options.delayed_impact: Three months later, a programme review finds that the 340 people with disabilities were never registered — they received no assistance for the entire programme.

options.label: Inclusive participatory assessment co-led by KCV with disaggregated groups
options.description_summary: Partner with KCV for a 3-day structured process: community circles in Kailani language and Tevita dialect, a dedicated session for people with disabilities, a women-only session, a youth group. Rights information shared before each session. Consent sought for any documentation.
options.chs_learning_note: Meaningful participation takes time but produces better data, builds trust, and establishes the precedent for community voice throughout the programme.
options.consequence_summary: Richer data. Disability section's specific needs are documented for the first time. Tevita community reveals a critical unmet need: clean water is the top priority, not food — an assumption that would have led to wrong distribution.
options.delayed_impact: KCV becomes a trusted co-implementer for all future community-facing work. Communities actively participate in monitoring six months later.

options.label: Digital mobile survey through camp leaders
options.description_summary: Deploy a mobile survey app through phone-owning community leaders. Fast and data-rich for those with phones. Sione flags that smartphone ownership in the camp is under 30%.
requirements_missed: 1.1 — systematically excludes elderly, disabled, non-phone owners
1.2 — no rights information in the app flow
options.chs_learning_note: Digital tools can accelerate or entrench exclusion. Tech-first approaches must be checked against who is left out.
options.consequence_summary: The 30% with phones are mostly young men — an economically active but not most-vulnerable segment. The assessment looks complete but systematically misrepresents the camp's needs.
options.delayed_impact: Distribution lists based on phone-owner networks miss the most vulnerable. A community elder tells KCV: 'We thought they didn't know we existed.'

options.label: Consult elected community council leaders only
options.description_summary: Meet with the three elected camp community councils — fast, respected, and locally legitimate. Council representatives are all men over 50.
requirements_missed: 1.1 — women, youth, disability groups not directly represented
options.chs_learning_note: Existing community structures are important but may not represent all voices. Consulting leaders without checking who they represent is a common gap.
options.consequence_summary: The assessment is respected by the councils, but a women's group leader later tells KCV that no women were consulted and the programme design reflects it.
options.delayed_impact: When a complaint is later filed about gender-blind distribution criteria, it can be traced directly to the assessment gap.

scenes.narrative:
The government official spreads the survey on the table before Maya has finished her coffee. "English, twenty questions, three hours," he says. "My enumerators know this camp."

Sione catches Maya's eye. "This camp has three different communities," he says, quietly but clearly. "Coastal Kailani, inland Tevita dialect speakers, and a section of about three hundred and forty elderly and disabled people at the far end — almost no outsiders go there. A written English survey won't reach any of them meaningfully."

Filomena steps forward, tablet in hand. "KCV can co-facilitate. Three days: community circles in both languages, a dedicated session for the disability section, a women-only group. We share their rights with them before we ask anything. Consent before data."

"Headquarters wants a preliminary assessment by end of day," Maya says, hearing the words as she speaks them. Sione looks at the ground. Filomena waits. The official recaps his pen.

Three days versus three hours. Everyone heard. No one heard. Maya makes her choice.

scenes.title: Invisible and Unheard
scenes.narrative_summary: The survey returns 620 responses — impressive on paper. But the disability section was inaccessible to enumerators without a ramp, Tevita dialect speakers couldn't understand the questions, and the women's section was visited only briefly. When Carlos produces the distribution plan, it is based on the main camp population, with no mention of 340 people who were simply never asked.
scenes.learning_point_summary: Rapid assessment methods that do not account for who is excluded produce data gaps that become programme gaps. The most marginalised are reliably the least reached.
scenes.chs_quote: Ensure diversity, equity and inclusion considerations are integrated into the organisation's work with people and communities, with attention to the most marginalised. (CHS 1.1)
scenes.image_prompt: Manu Displacement Camp, late afternoon. Maya and Carlos stand over a laptop showing a clean table: 620 survey responses, 100% distribution coverage against the approved list. Carlos points at the screen, satisfied. Behind them, through the open tent flap, a long concrete ramp leads to the disability section — no enumerator visible, the area quiet and unreached. To the side, a cluster of Tevita-dialect speakers exchange confused looks at a printed English form. Sione stands apart, field notebook pressed to his chest, expression unreadable. A single elderly man in a wheelchair sits alone at the camp perimeter, facing away. Flat digital illustration, fading afternoon light, ochre and grey palette.
scenes.narrative:
Carlos sets the laptop on the field table with a small satisfied motion. "Six hundred and twenty responses. Done by midday. HQ will be happy."

Maya scans the summary: coverage mapped against the government population list, every section ticked. It looks complete. It looks like a programme.

Sione reads over her shoulder, then walks to the tent entrance and looks toward the far corner of the camp. The disability section. She can see the concrete path that leads there — no enumerator footprints in the wet ground. "They didn't go in," he says. "No ramp access on their side. The team went around."

Filomena arrives with a single sheet of paper. "Forty-seven Tevita households. I asked them what the survey questions meant. None of them knew."

The number 340 sits in Maya's chest like a stone. Not a data gap. Three hundred and forty people who were never asked a single question. Three months from now, a programme review will confirm they received no assistance at all.

scenes.title: Heard — and the Data Shows It
scenes.narrative_summary: The KCV-led process takes 3 days. HQ is frustrated. But the findings are transformative: the disability section reveals nine families who need modified shelter kits; the Tevita-language session uncovers that clean water is the top priority, overriding the pre-planned food-first approach; women's group participants report that distribution in mixed-gender public spaces is unsafe for them. Maya revises the entire programme plan.
scenes.learning_point_summary: Participatory needs assessment — designed for inclusion — produces data that single-method surveys never reach. The three days saved months of misdirected programming.
scenes.chs_quote: Ensure people's participation in decisions and actions is meaningful for them and corresponds to their preferred ways of engaging. (CHS 1.4)
scenes.image_prompt: Manu Displacement Camp, end of Day 4. A community dialogue circle is in progress under a large shade shelter: Filomena Vaka facilitates in the foreground, a mixed group of women, elders, youth, and wheelchair users seated in the circle, all visibly engaged. Sione translates quietly at the edge. Maya sits beside a table covered in handwritten flip-chart notes, a map of the camp with disaggregated data pencilled in. One note reads: 'Water — TOP PRIORITY (Tevita).' Another: 'Modified shelter kits — 9 families.' A third: 'Women-only distribution space needed.' Through the shelter's open side: the disability section is visible and active for the first time. Flat digital illustration, warm late-afternoon Pacific light, blues and warm greens.
scenes.narrative:
Headquarters sends a second reminder on Day 3. Maya reads it and puts her phone face-down.

On Day 4, Filomena presents the findings on flip-chart paper pinned to the shelter wall. The disability section: nine families documented for the first time, their specific shelter needs recorded in careful handwriting. The Tevita-language session: a revelation. The community's top priority is not food — it is clean water. The pre-planned food-first approach would have been wrong from the start.

Sione reads aloud from his notes. "Women's group asked that distribution not happen in the main square. They said mixed-gender public spaces feel unsafe for them."

Maya crosses out two lines of the programme plan and begins writing new ones. Three days spent. Months of misdirected programming avoided.

"This is what we needed," she says. Filomena nods. It is not a compliment — it is a fact. KCV becomes the trusted co-implementer for every community-facing step that follows.

scenes.title: The Digital Divide
scenes.narrative_summary: The app survey returns 412 responses in 90 minutes. Amara reviews the respondent demographics: 81% male, 74% aged 18-45, 0% from the disability section. The data is clean and fast — and deeply unrepresentative. When Sione points this out, Carlos argues the sample is 'statistically significant.' Maya has to decide whether to flag the gap or proceed.
scenes.learning_point_summary: Digital tools reflect the inequalities of the communities that use them. Mobile-first approaches without offline alternatives systematically exclude those with least access.
scenes.chs_quote: Communicate in languages and formats that are easily accessible, understandable, respectful and contextually appropriate for people and communities. (CHS 1.3)
scenes.image_prompt: HopeForward field office, 90 minutes after the digital survey launch. Amara sits at a laptop displaying a demographic breakdown dashboard: a stark bar chart showing 81% male respondents, 74% aged 18–45, a bold zero in the disability section column. Carlos stands behind her, arms folded, half-smiling — 412 responses, fast and tidy. Maya stands to the side, arms at her sides, looking at the zero. Through the window: the camp is visible. A group of elderly women sit outside the disability section with no phones in sight. Sione stands at the office door, field notebook open to a page with a single underlined question: 'Who was left out?' Flat digital illustration, harsh midday light, blue-grey office palette contrasting with warm camp colours outside.
scenes.narrative:
Ninety minutes. Four hundred and twelve responses. Carlos sends the summary to HQ before Maya has finished reading it.

Amara opens the demographic breakdown quietly. She does not say anything at first. She just rotates the laptop so Maya can see the screen: 81% male respondents, 74% aged 18 to 45. The disability section column reads zero.

"The sample is statistically significant," Carlos says. "Coverage looks solid."

"Significant for whom?" Sione asks from the doorway. "The 30% with smartphones in this camp are mostly young men. We've surveyed the most economically mobile people here. We've built a programme plan for them."

Maya thinks of the elderly women she passed this morning near the camp's quiet corner, none of them holding phones. The data is clean. It is also a systematic record of who was easiest to reach — not who needed to be heard. Six months later, a community elder will tell KCV: "We thought they didn't know we existed."

scenes.title: The View from the Top of the Hierarchy
scenes.narrative_summary: The council sessions are efficient and productive. The three council chairs are articulate and community-minded. But when KCV's Filomena reviews the findings, she asks Maya: 'Did you meet any women? Any young people? Anyone with a disability?' The answer is no. The assessment reflects the community — but only the part the council was designed to represent.
scenes.learning_point_summary: Community leadership structures are legitimate and important — but they do not automatically represent all community members. Inclusion requires going beyond existing hierarchies.
scenes.chs_quote: Ensure diversity, equity and inclusion considerations are integrated into the organisation's work with people and communities, with attention to the most marginalised. (CHS 1.1)
scenes.image_prompt: Manu Camp community hall, morning. Maya sits at a table with three male council chairs — all men over 50, each with a visible leadership symbol: a carved staff, a formal sash, a government lanyard. The meeting is cordial and productive; papers are being signed. But through the hall's open doorway, Filomena Vaka stands in the threshold, arms folded, watching. Her expression is quiet but pointed. Behind Filomena: a group of camp women gathered outside, not invited, watching from a distance. A young woman with a disability sits on a low wall nearby, also excluded. Flat digital illustration, morning light through louvred windows, warm brown tones, institutional setting contrasting with the excluded figures in the doorway.
scenes.narrative:
The council meeting takes seventy minutes. All three chairs are articulate, community-minded, and generous with their time. By the end, Maya has four pages of notes and a handshake from each man.

Outside, Filomena is waiting. She doesn't sit down.

"Did you speak with any women?" she asks.

Maya hesitates.

"Any young people? Anyone from the disability section?"

"The councils represent the whole community," Maya says.

Filomena opens her notebook. "These three chairs were elected by adult male household heads. That's how the council structure works here. The women's group has a separate leadership — they weren't informed this meeting was happening." She pauses. "The disability section has no council representation at all."

The assessment is locally legitimate and socially respected. It reflects the community — the part of it that the formal hierarchy was built to represent. When a complaint about gender-blind distribution criteria is filed three months later, the gap can be traced directly to today.

scenes.chapter: Chapter 2 — The Right Help at the Right Time
scenes.title: Decision 2 — What Do You Actually Distribute, and How?

chs_commitment.title: People and communities access timely and effective support in accordance with their specific needs and priorities.

all_requirements.2.1: Plan and implement programmes which respect and build upon local knowledge, capacities and existing actions.
all_requirements.2.2: Use fair, impartial and transparent criteria to define programmes and the people or groups supported by the organisation.
all_requirements.2.3: Regularly monitor and adjust programmes to ensure actions are timely, accessible and address the priority needs of people and communities.
all_requirements.2.4: Apply relevant technical standards and recognised good practice in the organisation's work with people and communities.
all_requirements.2.5: Refer any unmet priority needs to relevant stakeholders with the technical expertise and capacity to address them.
all_requirements.2.6: Establish a coherent organisational approach to ensure support is based on an understanding of the context and culture and the diverse capacities, vulnerabilities, needs and risks faced by people and communities, with attention to the most marginalised.

scenes.situation_summary: Week 1. Carlos has the logistics plan ready: distribute pre-positioned high-energy biscuits and plastic sheeting to all registered households — the standard emergency package. If inclusive_assessment was conducted, Sione's data shows the top priority is clean water and dignity kits; the disability section needs modified shelter kits; and Tevita Village is not on the distribution list at all — they were registered by a different agency months ago but never received anything. The donor has approved the HEB and sheeting plan. Sphere standards apply. An unmet nutrition need for children under 5 is apparent but HopeForward has no WASH or nutrition expertise in-country.

context_flag_effects.inclusive_assessment: Sione has clear disaggregated data on specific needs by group. Filomena from KCV has offered community-specific distribution lists.
context_flag_effects.no_flag: Assessment data is weak. Carlos is working from government population estimates that are 6 months old.

scenes.image_prompt: HopeForward logistics warehouse, Port Hana, Day 5 after Cyclone Vera. Carlos Reyes stands at a long metal shelving unit, one hand resting on a stack of HEB boxes, the other holding a tablet showing a colour-coded distribution timeline. His expression is tight with urgency. Maya Chen stands opposite, arms folded, looking at a whiteboard she has half-covered with community priority notes: 'WATER — top priority (Tevita),' 'Dignity kits,' 'Modified shelter kits — disability section,' 'Tevita Village — NOT on list.' A printed donor approval letter is pinned to the corkboard behind Carlos. Sione stands to Maya's left, field notebook open to a disaggregated needs table with rows for each community group. On a side table, a phone shows a missed-call notification labelled 'Donor Rep — M. Fischer.' Flat digital illustration, cool warehouse fluorescent light, blue-grey and amber palette.

options.label: Distribute pre-positioned stocks as planned — no changes
options.description_summary: Move HEBs and plastic sheeting to registered households exactly as planned. Fast, donor-compliant, logistically clean.
requirements_missed: 2.2 — Tevita Village excluded with no transparent reason
options.chs_learning_note: Distributing what is available rather than what is needed is the most common programme quality failure in emergency response.
options.consequence_summary: Distribution happens in 6 hours. Community members trade HEBs at the market for water containers. Tevita Village receives nothing. No monitoring is built in to catch the problem.
options.delayed_impact: At week 4, a community feedback session reveals HEBs were mostly resold. The disability section received plastic sheeting they cannot use without physical assistance to install it. No record of what was actually needed.

options.label: Adapt distribution: community priorities, fair criteria, monitoring built in
options.description_summary: Delay 48 hours. Negotiate with the donor for partial flexibility (HEBs → water purification tablets and dignity kits; modified kits for disability section). Publish transparent targeting criteria. Add Tevita Village to the list. Build a simple weekly monitoring checklist. Refer the child nutrition gap to UNICEF.
options.chs_learning_note: Taking two extra days to adapt a programme saves weeks of misdirected effort and protects the dignity of the people being assisted.
options.consequence_summary: Distribution takes 48 extra hours. Tevita Village is reached. The disability section receives modified kits. UNICEF extends their nutrition programme to cover the child nutrition gap. Community satisfaction at week 4 is the highest across the cluster.
options.delayed_impact: The monitoring system Maya set up catches a distribution bottleneck in week 3, allowing a rapid correction. Donor increases flexible funding for phase 2 based on quality evidence.

options.label: Use an external consultant's generic emergency package
options.description_summary: HQ has a preferred external consultant with a ready-made emergency response package based on a regional template. Quick to deploy; technically sound by regional standards.
requirements_missed: 2.2 — targeting criteria from regional template, not community data
2.6 — ignores Kailani-specific context, dialect groups, local economy
options.chs_learning_note: Templates and regional standards are starting points, not substitutes for local knowledge. CHS 2.1 and 2.6 require context-specific programme design.
options.consequence_summary: The package is rolled out efficiently. But the consultant's template targets 'general displaced households' — which misses the specific vulnerabilities of Kailani's communal land structures, where families share resources in ways the individual-household model doesn't capture.
options.delayed_impact: Several wealthier households receive multiple distributions because they have more registered adults; the most vulnerable extended families receive proportionally less.

options.label: Distribute equally to everyone, no targeting
options.description_summary: Remove targeting criteria entirely — give one standard package to every displaced person registered in the camp, regardless of vulnerability or specific need.
requirements_missed: 2.2 — actually fails equity: equal is not equitable when needs differ
options.chs_learning_note: Equal distribution is not the same as equitable distribution. When needs differ, equal treatment can deepen inequality. CHS 2.2 requires criteria that reflect actual vulnerability.
options.consequence_summary: No one is left off the list — which feels fair. But the 340 people with disabilities receive the same package as able-bodied adults, and Tevita Village, not yet registered, still gets nothing.
options.delayed_impact: A community elder with a severe disability tells KCV he received a 20kg plastic sheeting roll he cannot lift. The distribution was equal; it was not just.

scenes.narrative:
Carlos is already pulling the warehouse door open when Maya arrives. "HEBs and sheeting, six hundred registered households, delivery starts at seven tomorrow." He taps his tablet: the logistics chain is green all the way down. "Donor approval came through at midnight. If we wait, we lose the truck convoy."

Sione steps forward quietly and opens his field notebook to a page of disaggregated data. If the inclusive assessment was completed, the numbers are precise: water purification ranked first by Tevita households; nine families in the disability section flagged structural shelter needs; Tevita Village — forty-seven households — absent from the registry entirely. "They're not on the approved list," Sione says. "They won't receive anything."

Maya's phone buzzes. Donor representative M. Fischer. She lets it ring once, then answers. "The HEB and sheeting budget has been approved," Fischer says. "Any material changes require a formal amendment — that takes five to seven working days." A pause. "I'd strongly encourage you to proceed as planned."

Carlos watches Maya. The trucks are booked. The donor is waiting. The community data says something different.

scenes.title: Boxes Ticked, Needs Missed
scenes.narrative_summary: Carlos's report shows 100% distribution rate against the approved list. The numbers are clean. But in the camp, HEBs are trading at the makeshift market for 20% of their face value — community members need water and soap, not high-energy biscuits. The disability section's plastic sheeting is stacked in a corner, unusable without the physical assistance no one provided.
scenes.learning_point_summary: Distribution against plan is not the same as meeting community needs. Efficiency metrics can mask programme irrelevance.
scenes.chs_quote: Plan and implement programmes which respect and build upon local knowledge, capacities and existing actions. (CHS 2.1)
scenes.image_prompt: Manu Camp distribution point, morning, Day 6 after Cyclone Vera. Two HopeForward staff carry HEB boxes off a truck and stack them on a trestle table. A short queue of registered household heads collects packages. In the foreground, a woman with a disability sits outside the distribution tent; a large plastic sheeting roll leans against the wall beside her — she cannot lift it alone. In the background, at the camp perimeter, a cluster of Tevita Village residents watch from a distance with empty hands — no table, no queue, no aid. Carlos stands to one side, marking names off a tablet checklist, expression satisfied. A makeshift market stall behind the main tent shows HEB packets trading for water containers. Flat digital illustration, harsh morning light, muted ochre and grey.
scenes.narrative:
Carlos's report lands in Maya's inbox before breakfast: 100% distribution rate against the approved list. The Sphere minimum for non-food items — 3.5 square metres of plastic sheeting per person — is met on paper. But at the distribution point, Sione watches a Tevita household member trade two HEB packets at the makeshift market for a plastic water container worth three times their value. Biscuits do not address a WASH deficit. Sphere's Water, Sanitation and Hygiene minimum — 15 litres of safe water per person per day — remains unmet, and no referral has been made to a WASH actor.

In the disability section, a 20-kilogram sheeting roll sits against the wall. The family received the Sphere-standard quantity. They received it in a format they cannot install without physical assistance that was never arranged. Requirement 2.6 — attention to the most marginalised — was not activated. Tevita Village, absent from the list, receives nothing. By week four, community feedback confirms that the distribution met the plan and missed the need.

scenes.title: The Right Things, To the Right People, At the Right Time
scenes.narrative_summary: The 48-hour delay frustrates Carlos and HQ. But when Amara runs the week-4 monitoring check, community satisfaction ratings are exceptional. Tevita Village received its first assistance. Modified kits reached the disability section. UNICEF is now covering the child nutrition gap. The donor's programme officer calls Maya personally to commend the adaptive approach.
scenes.learning_point_summary: Adapting programmes to actual needs — even when it creates short-term friction — produces the outcomes the programme exists to achieve.
scenes.chs_quote: Regularly monitor and adjust programmes to ensure actions are timely, accessible and address the priority needs of people and communities. (CHS 2.3)
scenes.image_prompt: Manu Camp distribution point, morning, Day 8 after Cyclone Vera. A well-organised, sector-divided distribution is underway. One lane shows water purification tablets and dignity kits; a second has modified shelter kits being handed to disability section families with a staff member assisting. A third lane at the far end shows Tevita Village residents — new arrivals — receiving packages for the first time. Amara stands to one side with a tablet, entering data into a weekly monitoring checklist. A handwritten sign reads: 'Targeting criteria — visible and public.' In the distance, a UNICEF vehicle pulls into the camp gate for the nutrition extension. Carlos leans against a truck, arms folded, expression tight but resigned. Flat digital illustration, clear morning light, blues and warm greens.
scenes.narrative:
The 48-hour delay is uncomfortable. Carlos files a formal logistics note — "convoy rescheduled, HQ notified" — and does not hide his frustration. The donor's amendment process is triggered for the partial reallocation from HEBs to water purification tablets and dignity kits, with the Sphere WASH minimum of 15 litres per person per day cited as the technical basis.

On Day 8, the distribution runs in three parallel lanes. Tevita Village — forty-seven households, previously invisible to the registry — receives assistance for the first time. Modified kits reach the disability section; installation support was arranged with a community volunteer. The transparent targeting criteria, posted at the distribution point per CHS 2.2, draw no complaints. UNICEF confirms the child nutrition referral (CHS 2.5) and extends their programme to cover the under-five gap. By week four, Amara's monitoring checklist — built to CHS 2.3 standards — flags a distribution bottleneck at the northern end of camp, corrected within 48 hours. Programme quality scores are the highest across the cluster.

scenes.title: Efficient, but Whose Context?
scenes.narrative_summary: The consultant's package is deployed in 4 hours. Technical quality is high — the consultant knows Sphere standards well. But the targeting algorithm flags 47 households in Tevita as 'already served' (by a different agency, six months ago, with a different package). They receive nothing. The regional template has no field for communal land structures.
scenes.learning_point_summary: Technical competence and contextual knowledge are not the same thing. Regional templates must be adapted to local realities, not applied wholesale.
scenes.chs_quote: Establish a coherent organisational approach to ensure support is based on an understanding of the context and culture. (CHS 2.6)
scenes.image_prompt: HopeForward field office, Port Hana, Day 6 after Cyclone Vera. A projector displays a consultant's regional template on a white wall: neatly colour-coded rows, a standardised 'general displaced household' targeting category, Sphere-standard quantities for food and shelter. The consultant — a professional in a polo shirt — gestures confidently at the table. Sione stands apart, pointing at a line on a printed camp map: Tevita Village, flagged as 'already served — GAP agency, six months prior.' His expression is flat. Behind him, a handwritten note on the whiteboard reads: 'Communal land structure — no individual-household field in template.' Maya sits at the table, pen in hand, looking from the template to Sione's map. Flat digital illustration, projector blue-white light, cool and clinical palette.
scenes.narrative:
The consultant's package deploys in four hours. Sphere standards for emergency food and non-food items are met — the regional template is technically rigorous. Carlos is relieved. But Sione flags a problem on Day 2.

The targeting algorithm marks 47 Tevita households as 'already served' — a GAP agency distribution from six months before the cyclone, with a different package, for a different need. The flag has not been cleared. Requirement 2.1 — building on local knowledge — was bypassed; the consultant had no brief on Kailani's communal land structures, where extended families share resources across registered households. Wealthier households with more registered adults receive proportionally more. The most vulnerable extended families receive less. The regional template had no field to capture this. Requirement 2.6 — contextual understanding of diversity and risk — was not applied. No monitoring mechanism was built in to catch the discrepancy; by week three, KCV's Filomena is the first to report it.

scenes.title: Equal Isn't Always Just
scenes.narrative_summary: Everyone on the list gets the same package. This is administratively simple and politically defensible. A community leader praises HopeForward's 'fairness.' But Amara's monitoring data tells a different story: households with elderly and disabled members report the standard package is unsuitable for their needs; single mothers with multiple children received the same as childless adult couples. Equity requires differentiation.
scenes.learning_point_summary: Equal treatment produces unequal outcomes when people's needs and circumstances differ. Equitable distribution requires fair criteria that reflect actual vulnerability.
scenes.chs_quote: Use fair, impartial and transparent criteria to define programmes and the people or groups supported by the organisation. (CHS 2.2)
scenes.image_prompt: Manu Camp main distribution square, Day 6 after Cyclone Vera. A single distribution lane stretches across the square: every person in the registration queue receives the same standard package — one HEB box, one plastic sheeting roll. A community leader stands beside a HopeForward banner, smiling. A printed sign reads: 'One package per registered person — fair for all.' In the foreground, an elderly man in a wheelchair holds the registration slip for his package; a 20-kilogram plastic sheeting roll sits beside him on the ground. He looks at it. He cannot lift it. Sione stands at a distance, field notebook open, writing. Tevita Village residents remain outside the camp boundary — still unregistered, still invisible. Flat digital illustration, flat midday light, neutral palette with one detail of warm colour on the wheelchair, isolated.
scenes.narrative:
The decision is administratively clean. One package per registered person: the Sphere minimum for non-food items, applied uniformly. No targeting criteria to defend, no community group left explicitly excluded. A community leader publicly commends HopeForward's fairness at the distribution point. Carlos sends a one-line update to HQ: 'Distribution complete. Zero complaints received.'

Amara's monitoring data tells a different story. Households with elderly and disabled members report the standard 20-kilogram sheeting roll is impossible to install without assistance that was never provided — CHS 2.6 requires attention to the most marginalised, and equal treatment has produced unequal outcomes. Single mothers with multiple children received the same package as childless couples. The disability section's assessed need for modified kits — documented in Sione's data — was set aside to avoid differential treatment. Tevita Village, unregistered, receives nothing. By week four, a community elder tells KCV: 'The distribution was equal. It was not just.'

scenes.chapter: Chapter 3 — Building for the Future or the Present?
scenes.title: Decision 3 — How Should the Programme Strengthen Local Resilience?

chs_commitment.title: People and communities are better prepared and more resilient to potential crises.

all_requirements.3.1: Support formal and informal community leadership and locally led efforts to reinforce the resilience of people and communities.
all_requirements.3.2: Support local capacities to anticipate and reduce risks of potential crises or disasters.
all_requirements.3.3: Plan and implement programmes that contribute to long-term positive effects on people's lives, livelihoods, the local economy and the environment.
all_requirements.3.4: Support local ownership of resources and decision making from the outset of work with people and communities.
all_requirements.3.5: Establish a coherent organisational approach to ensure support reinforces locally led actions and decision making.

scenes.situation_summary: Week 2. HQ is asking Maya to choose a programme delivery model for the 6-month response. Filomena from KCV has proposed a co-governance model where KCV chairs a Community Programme Committee that approves all major decisions — including budget allocations. HopeForward's HQ prefers a 'direct delivery' model (faster, easier to audit). The Kailani Meteorological Service has flagged that cyclone season is not over — another storm system is forming. No community-based early warning system exists. The existing programme plan makes no mention of disaster risk reduction. Carlos says embedding DRR will delay distribution by one week. Sione says the community has deep traditional knowledge about storm warning signs — it just needs to be documented and formally linked to a response plan.

context_flag_effects.inclusive_assessment: KCV and community leaders are already trusted partners. A co-governance proposal is more realistic and welcomed.
context_flag_effects.no_flag: Community trust with KCV is lower; direct delivery will face less internal opposition.

scenes.image_prompt: HopeForward's field office, Week 2. Maya stands at a whiteboard with two columns: 'Direct Delivery' and 'Community-Led Governance'. A laptop screen shows a HQ video call — the regional director's face tight with impatience. Across the table, Filomena Tui holds a laminated co-governance proposal. Sione Taufa sits to one side, a notepad of hand-drawn storm-sign symbols in his lap. Through the window, a weather app on a phone displays a new tropical depression forming. Maps pinned to the wall show Manu Camp and the coastline. Flat digital illustration, morning light, tension between urgency and possibility, relief-blue and ochre palette.

options.label: Direct delivery model — HopeForward controls all decisions and resources
options.description_summary: Implement HopeForward's standard direct delivery model. Faster to establish; easier for HQ to audit. Community are recipients, not decision-makers.
requirements_missed: 3.4 — no local ownership of resources or decisions
3.5 — no organisational approach to local leadership
options.chs_learning_note: Operational control feels efficient, but it creates dependency and undermines local systems. When HopeForward leaves, nothing is stronger than before.
options.consequence_summary: The programme runs efficiently for 6 months. Communities receive assistance. But when HopeForward's funding ends, KCV lacks the governance capacity to sustain anything, and the community has no disaster early warning system when the next tropical storm arrives.
options.delayed_impact: An independent evaluation 12 months later finds: 'HopeForward delivered services effectively but left no strengthened local systems. This was a missed opportunity for resilience-building.'

options.label: Co-governance from day one, with KCV chairing the Community Programme Committee
options.description_summary: Accept Filomena's co-governance model. KCV chairs a community committee with real decision-making authority over programme priorities and budget allocations. HopeForward provides technical support and resources.
options.chs_learning_note: Local ownership from the outset — including resource control — is the most effective route to sustainable programming.
options.consequence_summary: The first committee meeting reveals community priorities that Maya had not considered: a local fishing cooperative needs a small grant to rebuild nets — a livelihoods investment that the direct delivery model would never have authorised.
options.delayed_impact: By month 6, KCV is running three programme components independently. When HopeForward exits, the community programme committee continues to meet monthly.

options.label: Integrate DRR into programme design — community early warning system
options.description_summary: Delay distribution by one week to co-design a community early warning system based on Sione's traditional knowledge mapping. Link it to the national meteorological service. Embed DRR activities alongside the emergency response.
options.chs_learning_note: Integrating disaster risk reduction into emergency response is cost-effective and life-saving. Traditional knowledge is a resource, not a quaint add-on.
options.consequence_summary: Six weeks later, the new tropical storm system intensifies. The community early warning system — linked to traditional observation and the met service — gives 18 hours warning. Eighteen families in the lowest-risk zone evacuate before flooding. No casualties.
options.delayed_impact: The early warning system continues to function after HopeForward exits. The Kailani Meteorological Service adopts the community-validation model nationally.

options.label: Co-governance AND DRR integration — full resilience-by-design approach
options.description_summary: Combine 3B and 3C: establish community co-governance, integrate DRR, embed livelihoods, transfer resources to local actors. Slower to establish — 10 extra days. Requires HQ buy-in.
options.chs_learning_note: The most transformative approach addresses all aspects of resilience simultaneously. It is harder but it is what CHS commitment 3 asks for.
options.consequence_summary: The 10-day setup period is tense — HQ pushes back hard, and the team is stretched. But by month 2, the community committee is making real decisions, the DRR system is live, and livelihoods activities have begun.
options.delayed_impact: At exit, the community programme committee, the early warning system, and the fishing cooperative grant programme all continue independently. An independent evaluator rates it the most sustainable response in the cluster.

scenes.title: Delivered but Not Strengthened
scenes.narrative_summary: For six weeks the programme runs to schedule. Distributions complete on time, HopeForward branding is visible at every distribution point, and Carlos submits weekly donor reports without a red flag. HQ is satisfied. Then, in week 7, the Kailani Meteorological Service issues a tropical storm warning for the coast. Maya looks for a community response plan: there is none. She looks for an evacuation route map: the file is blank. KCV has no emergency contact list beyond three HopeForward staff names. HopeForward vehicles are loaded with equipment and depart the camp by 6pm. The community — twelve thousand displaced people — waits with no early warning system, no local coordination structure, and no information. The storm veers north. This time. The six-month evaluation reads: 'Efficient delivery; zero investment in community resilience.'
scenes.learning_point_summary: Effective emergency delivery and building local resilience are not mutually exclusive — but they require deliberate design. Operational efficiency without resilience-building is a missed obligation.
scenes.chs_quote: Support local ownership of resources and decision making from the outset of work with people and communities. (CHS 3.4)
scenes.image_prompt: Manu Camp field office, evening, Week 7. Maya sits at a table with a blank evacuation-routes folder open in front of her. Carlos is loading HopeForward equipment boxes onto a vehicle visible through the open doorway. On the laptop screen: a satellite image of a tropical storm system approaching the Kailani coast. Through the window, twelve thousand displaced people are visible waiting outside their shelters — no visible community leader, no signs, no coordination. A HopeForward banner hangs undisturbed. The tone is eerie calm before a storm. Flat digital illustration, dusk light, grey and ochre palette.
scenes.narrative:
Carlos sets the last crate in the vehicle and brushes his hands clean. "HQ is happy," he says. "Six distributions, zero complaints filed, reports submitted on time."

Maya opens the emergency response folder. It contains one page: a list of three HopeForward staff phone numbers. No community contact tree. No evacuation map. No early warning protocol.

The Kailani Met Service alert arrived forty minutes ago. Tropical depression forming. Intensification likely.

She looks out at the camp. Twelve thousand people. No one is doing anything because no one knows what to do. KCV's Filomena was never given authority to organise a response. Sione's traditional knowledge about storm behaviour — the cloud formations, the sea colour, the specific bird patterns he mentioned in Week 2 — was never documented.

The vehicles pull out at 6pm.

The storm veers north at 11pm. No casualties. Forty shelters damaged. The evaluation report, twelve months later, will note: 'Operational efficiency; no strengthened local systems. A missed obligation.'

context_flag_effects.no_flag: HopeForward retains full programme authority. No community governance structure is established. At Decision 6 (Coordination), HopeForward cannot propose KCV as a cluster co-lead — there is no documentation of shared decision-making. At Decision 9 (Exit Planning), no local system exists to cite as evidence of sustainable capacity. The community has no early warning infrastructure when future climate events occur.

scenes.delayed_impact: An independent evaluation 12 months later finds: 'HopeForward delivered services effectively but left no strengthened local systems. This was a missed opportunity for resilience-building.'

scenes.title: Ownership in Practice
scenes.narrative_summary: The first Community Programme Committee meeting is loud and overlapping — four conversations at once, Filomena writing minutes across multiple threads, Carlos checking his watch from the doorway. By the third meeting, the committee has voted to redirect 8% of the flexible budget to rebuild the fishing cooperative's nets. Maya does not authorise it. Filomena does, as committee chair. The fishing families, now co-owners of a functioning livelihood, organise their own distribution volunteers in week 5 without being asked. When HQ queries the budget variance, Maya sends the committee minutes as programme documentation. By month 4, KCV is leading two programme components independently. The dependency that direct delivery would have guaranteed is, quietly, not forming.
scenes.learning_point_summary: Local ownership means real authority over real resources — not consultation after decisions are made. When communities control decisions, they identify solutions that outsiders never would.
scenes.chs_quote: Support local ownership of resources and decision making from the outset of work with people and communities. (CHS 3.4)
scenes.image_prompt: Community hall, Manu Camp, Week 3. The Community Programme Committee meeting is in progress: Filomena Tui at the head of the table, writing on a large notepad, visibly managing several conversations simultaneously. Six community members — fishing cooperative representatives, a women's group leader, a youth delegate — are talking at once. Carlos stands in the doorway, arms folded, watching. Maya sits to one side, notably not at the head of the table — an observer, not the chair. A whiteboard shows a budget breakdown with '8% — nets, fishing coop' circled. The energy is productive chaos, not crisis. Flat digital illustration, daytime indoor light, blues and warm ochre palette.

context_flag_effects.local_ownership_by_design: KCV's established governance role reduces friction in all subsequent co-implementation decisions. HQ audits accept Community Programme Committee minutes as legitimate programme documentation. At Decision 6 (Coordination), HopeForward can propose KCV as a cluster co-lead without needing to establish their credibility from scratch.
context_flag_effects.no_flag: Default path: HopeForward retains full decision-making authority. Community consultation occurs but programme authority remains centralised. KCV functions as a service provider, not a governance partner.

scenes.narrative:
The first committee meeting is, by any objective measure, a disaster. Four people speak at the same time. Filomena writes in three columns simultaneously. Carlos checks his watch from the doorway with the expression of a man calculating how much this is costing per hour.

The second meeting is better. The third meeting is where it changes.

The committee votes — by show of hands — to redirect 8% of the flexible budget to rebuild the fishing cooperative's nets. Maya does not authorise it. Filomena authorises it, as committee chair, because that is what the governance model says she can do. Carlos raises an eyebrow. Maya says nothing.

In week 5, the fishing families organise their own distribution volunteers. Nobody asked them to. The committee planned it.

When HQ queries the budget variance, Maya sends the committee minutes. The variance is approved.

By month 4, KCV is running two programme components independently. The thing that was supposed to take a year is already happening.
scenes.delayed_impact: By month 6, KCV is running three programme components independently. When HopeForward exits, the community programme committee continues to meet monthly.

scenes.title: The Storm That Didn't Kill Anyone
scenes.narrative_summary: Week 7, 3:47am. The first early warning alert goes out — a WhatsApp broadcast combining Sione's traditional storm indicators (specific cloud formations, unusual sea colour, migratory bird behaviour) with the Kailani Meteorological Service's satellite data. By 5am, eighteen families in the lowest flood-risk zone have self-evacuated to the community hall on higher ground. By 6am, the flooding begins. In Manu Camp: zero casualties. On the Northern Coast, where no community early warning system exists: eleven people hospitalised. At 8am the Kailani Met Service director calls Maya to ask if HopeForward can document the community validation protocol. They want to replicate it nationally. Ten days of co-design in Week 2 is the difference between a natural hazard and a disaster.
scenes.learning_point_summary: Investing in local disaster risk reduction capacities during an emergency response is not a luxury — it is a core CHS obligation that saves lives.
scenes.chs_quote: Support local capacities to anticipate and reduce risks of potential crises or disasters. (CHS 3.2)
scenes.image_prompt: Manu Camp community hall, predawn, Week 7. Eighteen families — adults carrying children, elderly supported by younger relatives — move up the path toward higher ground, guided by hand-held torches. A community member holds a phone showing the WhatsApp early warning message. Sione stands at the hall entrance directing people in. In the background, below the hill, water is beginning to rise in the low-lying camp area. The sky is dark and turbulent. The contrast between orderly movement uphill and the spreading floodwater below is the image's central tension. Flat digital illustration, predawn blue-grey palette, torch-light warm accents.

context_flag_effects.drr_integrated: The community early warning system remains active after programme exit. At Decision 9 (Exit Planning), the DRR system is cited as evidence of sustainable local capacity — reducing pressure to extend the programme. The Kailani Met Service partnership provides ongoing technical validation.
context_flag_effects.no_flag: Default path: no community early warning capacity exists at exit. Future climate events reach the community without advance preparation. Any new response must rebuild risk-reduction capacity from zero.

scenes.narrative:
3:47am. The WhatsApp broadcast goes to 340 community members simultaneously.

The message combines two things: Sione's traditional storm indicators — the specific cloud stacking he described in Week 2, the unusual sea colour reported by the fishing cooperative, the migratory birds that shouldn't be here at this season — and the Kailani Met Service's satellite pressure readings, cross-referenced and confirmed.

It took ten days to co-design this system in Week 2. Carlos called it a delay.

By 5am, eighteen families in the flood zone have reached the community hall. The same hall that the committee — the one that doesn't exist in Option A's timeline — designated as the evacuation point. By 6am, the water is rising.

Zero casualties in Manu Camp.

On the Northern Coast: eleven people hospitalised. No early warning system there.

At 8am, the Kailani Met Service director calls. She wants the community validation protocol documented. They're going to replicate it for every coastal community in the archipelago.

Ten days. That was the investment.
scenes.delayed_impact: The early warning system continues to function after HopeForward exits. The Kailani Meteorological Service adopts the community-validation model nationally.

scenes.title: Built to Last
scenes.narrative_summary: Two strongly worded emails from HQ. One call with the regional director described internally as 'heated.' The ten-day setup period stretches the team. Then: week 3, the Community Programme Committee meets for the first time. Week 5, the committee approves the fishing cooperative grant independently — no HopeForward sign-off required. Week 7, Tropical Storm Beni intensifies. The early warning system activates at 3:47am; eighteen families self-evacuate; zero casualties. At the six-month programme review, the cluster coordinator uses the Kailani model as the benchmark for sustainable response. The regional director who complained about the delays asks Maya to write a case study. Filomena presents at a regional resilience conference. Three months after HopeForward exits, the committee still meets monthly.
scenes.learning_point_summary: Addressing all dimensions of resilience — governance, risk reduction, livelihoods, local ownership — requires more effort upfront but produces fundamentally different long-term outcomes.
scenes.chs_quote: Plan and implement programmes that contribute to long-term positive effects on people's lives, livelihoods, the local economy and the environment. (CHS 3.3)
scenes.image_prompt: Split scene, two moments in time. Left panel: Week 2 setup — Maya, Filomena, and Sione around a table covered in governance framework documents and traditional storm-knowledge sketches; Carlos in the background on a phone, expression strained; a laptop showing two strongly worded HQ emails. Right panel: Month 6 programme review — the same three figures, now at a cluster meeting table; the Community Programme Committee chair position clearly Filomena's; a map showing the early warning system coverage; the cluster coordinator pointing at the Kailani model on a presentation screen. Progression from tension to recognition. Flat digital illustration, warm Pacific light, blues and ochre palette.

context_flag_effects.local_ownership_by_design: KCV's co-governance role is formally documented. At Decision 6 (Coordination) HopeForward can propose KCV as cluster co-lead. HQ accepts committee minutes as programme governance documentation. Dependency risk is structurally reduced.
context_flag_effects.drr_integrated: The community early warning system provides ongoing protection after programme exit. At Decision 9 (Exit Planning), the integrated DRR infrastructure reduces pressure to extend the programme and demonstrates sustainable local capacity. The Kailani Met Service partnership continues independently.
context_flag_effects.both_flags_combined: The combination of community governance and DRR integration produces the highest long-term resilience score in the cluster. Both systems reinforce each other: the committee oversees DRR maintenance; the DRR system gives the committee a credible mandate beyond the programme cycle.

scenes.narrative:
HQ's second email arrives on Day 8. The subject line is: "RE: RE: Delivery Timeline — Escalation Required."

Maya reads it and files it.

The committee holds its first meeting in Week 3. It is loud and imperfect and completely, structurally real: Filomena is the chair, the budget line is there, the decision-making authority is written into the programme framework. Carlos watches from the doorway with the expression of someone who is not sure he was wrong but is starting to wonder.

Week 5: the committee approves the fishing cooperative grant. No one calls Maya. No one needs to.

Week 7, 3:47am: Tropical Storm Beni. The early warning system — part Sione's traditional indicators, part Kailani Met Service data, part the committee's designated evacuation protocol — activates. Eighteen families move to higher ground before the flooding begins. Zero casualties.

At the six-month review, the regional director — who sent the escalation email — asks Maya to write a case study.

Three months after HopeForward exits, the committee meets without anyone from HopeForward present. The early warning system is still active. The fishing cooperative is still running.

The evaluator's summary: 'Most sustainable response in the cluster.'
scenes.delayed_impact: At exit, the community programme committee, the early warning system, and the fishing cooperative grant programme all continue independently. An independent evaluator rates it the most sustainable response in the cluster.

scenes.title: Decision 4 — How Do You Protect People, Data, and the Environment?

chs_commitment.title: People and communities access support that does not cause harm to people or the environment.

all_requirements.4.1: Identify, prevent, mitigate and address potential and actual negative impacts of programmes on people and communities.
all_requirements.4.2: Identify, prevent, mitigate and address potential and actual negative impacts of programmes on the environment.
all_requirements.4.3: Ensure safe, ethical and effective management of data and information to minimise risks for people and communities in line with recognised good practice for data protection.
all_requirements.4.4: Establish a coherent organisational approach to ensure the organisation works in ways that protect the safety, security, rights and dignity of people and communities and prevent all forms of exploitation and abuse, including sexual exploitation, abuse and harassment, by staff and volunteers in line with recognised good practice.
all_requirements.4.5: Establish a coherent organisational approach to reduce the negative environmental impacts of the organisation and its work in line with recognised good practice.

scenes.situation_summary: Week 3. HopeForward is two days from scale-up. Four risk signals land on Maya's desk simultaneously. (1) A KCV field volunteer in Tevita Village reports that male relatives are intercepting women outside the cash-distribution shelter, pressuring them to hand over their allocations — two women who refused were followed home. (2) Amara flags overnight that the beneficiary database — 4,000 names, GPS coordinates, vulnerability classifications — is stored unencrypted in a shared Google Drive folder; the access password is 'HopeForward2024', printed in the footer of every registration form currently in the field. (3) Three written community complaints describe HopeForward's trucks idling three hours each morning at the camp water point while waiting for loading; children play there; one complaint includes a crayon drawing of a truck with black smoke and small stick figures nearby. (4) The onboarding log has a gap: no PSEA orientation has been conducted, no code of conduct signed, and no reporting pathway posted at any distribution point. Carlos says addressing all four will delay scale-up by one week and the funding window closes in six days.

context_flag_effects.inclusive_assessment: Communities are more likely to trust and report protection risks if they have already experienced respectful engagement.
context_flag_effects.community_informed: If rights were shared in Decision 1, some community members may already know they can report concerns.

scenes.image_prompt: Maya Chen at her field desk in the HopeForward operations tent, early morning, Week 3. Four printed notes are pinned to her laptop screen: 'Tevita — women intercepted, cash coercion'; 'Google Drive — 4,000 records, password=HopeForward2024'; 'Truck fumes — children at water point, 3 complaints'; 'PSEA — no orientation, no pathway'. Carlos Reyes stands to her right, pointing at a project Gantt chart on a printed A3 sheet, expression urgent, laptop open showing a countdown: 'SCALE-UP: T-6 DAYS'. Amara Diallo stands to her left holding a printed email, looking strained. Through the tent opening, a community water point is faintly visible with a truck parked nearby. Flat digital illustration, Pacific island humanitarian setting, relief-blue and ochre palette, sense of converging simultaneous pressures.

options.label: Scale up on schedule — address issues as they arise
options.description_summary: Proceed with the scale-up on schedule. Log the four issues as 'in progress' and address them in the next planning cycle.
options.chs_learning_note: Known risks that are not addressed become harm. Proceeding without mitigating identified risks is a choice — and the consequences fall on the people being served.
options.consequence_summary: Three weeks later: a confirmed PSEA incident involving a volunteer; a data breach when the Google Drive is accessed by an unknown party; two domestic coercion incidents in Tevita; and community members formally complain about the truck fumes.
options.delayed_impact: HopeForward faces a donor review triggered by the data breach. The PSEA incident becomes a global HQ investigation. The programme is suspended for 3 weeks.

options.label: Pause scale-up one week: address all four risks systematically
options.description_summary: Delay scale-up by one week. Address all four risks: modify cash delivery modality in Tevita; encrypt and access-restrict the database; reroute truck idling away from the water point; conduct mandatory PSEA orientation with all staff and volunteers. Share a brief protection framework with KCV.
options.chs_learning_note: Prevention is not bureaucracy — it is the ethical foundation of humanitarian action. All five requirements of CHS 4 can be addressed in parallel with adequate planning.
options.consequence_summary: The scale-up is one week late. No PSEA incidents are reported in the programme period. The data breach risk is eliminated. The modified cash delivery at Tevita reduces coercion incidents to zero. Community members notice the truck rerouting without being asked.
options.delayed_impact: When a protection concern is raised three months later, the PSEA reporting pathway already exists and is used correctly. The incident is addressed within 48 hours.

options.label: Address protection and data risks only; skip environmental review
options.description_summary: Fix the data breach and conduct PSEA orientation. Modify the cash modality in Tevita. The truck fumes issue is deprioritised — it is seen as a minor environmental concern that can wait.
options.chs_learning_note: Environmental harm is not an optional add-on to humanitarian programming. Children exposed to daily diesel fumes near their water point is a real harm, regardless of how it compares to other priorities.
options.consequence_summary: Protection and data risks are managed well. But two months later, a community health worker documents a pattern of respiratory complaints among children under 5 near the water point — traced to the truck idling pattern HopeForward chose not to address.
options.delayed_impact: The issue is eventually resolved but only after a community complaint is submitted. A Kailani environmental group publicises it on social media.

options.label: Address environmental and data risks only; handle protection issues informally
options.description_summary: Fix the data breach and reroute the trucks. Handle the Tevita cash coercion risk with a verbal briefing to the cash distribution team. Postpone formal PSEA orientation to the next monthly staff meeting.
options.chs_learning_note: Informal handling of PSEA risks is a known pathway to serious harm. CHS 4.4 requires a coherent organisational approach — not ad-hoc verbal briefings.
options.consequence_summary: The data is protected and the trucks are rerouted. The cash coercion risk in Tevita is partially addressed through informal briefings. One month later, a new distribution volunteer (not briefed) engages in conditional behaviour that is later reported.
options.delayed_impact: The informal approach to PSEA creates a patchwork of awareness — those who were verbally briefed acted appropriately; those who weren't did not. The complaint investigation reveals the gap.

scenes.narrative:
Carlos drops the project timeline on Maya's table at 7:45 a.m. "All six distribution zones go live next week. Nothing changes."

Maya opens four tabs on her laptop.

The first is a WhatsApp message forwarded by Sione — a KCV volunteer in Tevita Village has seen male relatives waiting outside the cash-distribution shelter, intercepting women as they leave. Two women who refused to hand over their allocation were followed home. "Early signal," Sione has typed. "Not confirmed as a pattern yet. But it's there."

The second is Amara's email, sent at 11:47 p.m. The beneficiary database — 4,000 names, GPS coordinates, vulnerability classifications — is sitting unencrypted in a shared Google Drive folder. The access password is 'HopeForward2024'. Amara has underlined the last line: "This password appears in the footer of every printed registration form currently in the field."

The third is a stack of three paper complaints from community members at the water point. HopeForward's trucks idle for three hours each morning while drivers wait for loading. Children play there. One complaint has a drawing clipped to it — a child's crayon sketch of a truck with black smoke, and small stick figures standing close.

The fourth is a gap. Maya scrolls the onboarding log: no PSEA orientation conducted, no code of conduct signed by any staff member or volunteer, no reporting pathway posted at any distribution point.

"One week," Maya says. "We'd need one week."

Carlos is already closing his laptop. "The funding window closes in six days. We don't have one week."

scenes.title: Four Risks, Four Harms
scenes.narrative_summary: Three weeks after scale-up, four harms arrive in sequence. An unknown session logs into the shared Google Drive at 2:14 a.m. and downloads the full beneficiary spreadsheet — 4,000 names, GPS coordinates, vulnerability classifications — before the connection drops. The password 'HopeForward2024' had been on every registration form. At the Sector 7 distribution point, two women independently report a volunteer for demanding a portion of their allocation before placing them in the queue. Sione finds no PSEA briefing record and no reporting pathway on any wall. In Tevita Village, two women have stopped attending distributions: 'My husband waits outside and takes the money when I leave. I stopped going.' Near the water point, a community health worker tallies eleven children under five with persistent coughs in the truck idling zone. The programme is suspended pending donor review.
scenes.learning_point_summary: Known risks that are not mitigated are not unknowns — they are choices. Every one of these harms was predictable and preventable. The cost of prevention would have been one week.
scenes.chs_quote: Identify, prevent, mitigate and address potential and actual negative impacts of programmes on people and communities. (CHS 4.1)
scenes.image_prompt: Four-panel split scene, each panel showing one materialised harm, arranged around a central HopeForward logo. Top left: a laptop screen showing a Google Drive access log — unknown IP, 2:14 a.m., file downloaded — Amara Diallo staring at the screen in the background, hand over mouth. Top right: Sione Taufa at a field table receiving a written complaint from two women at the Sector 7 distribution tent, a third woman watching from the entrance. Bottom left: two women in Tevita Village standing outside their shelter, arms folded, not walking toward the distribution point; a male figure is visible in shadow at the shelter entrance behind them. Bottom right: a community health worker kneeling next to a small child near the camp water point, tally sheet on clipboard showing eleven marks, a HopeForward truck idling in the background. Flat digital illustration, Pacific island humanitarian setting, muted and heavy palette.
scenes.narrative:
Carlos marks the scale-up launched on the project tracker. Week 6 begins.

The data breach is silent. No one at HopeForward notices until a partner agency emails to say names and GPS coordinates from the beneficiary list have appeared in a Facebook group message — posted by an account no one recognises, asking if anyone can identify the addresses. Amara pulls the Drive access log: an unknown login at 2:14 a.m., thirteen minutes, the full spreadsheet downloaded. The password was 'HopeForward2024'. It was printed on every registration form distributed in the field.

The PSEA complaint arrives with a name. A volunteer at the Sector 7 distribution point. Two women reported him independently. Sione reviews the onboarding file: no PSEA briefing record, no code of conduct signature, no reporting pathway posted anywhere.

In Tevita Village, Filomena finds the two women who stopped attending distributions at home. 'My husband waits outside the shelter,' one says quietly. 'He takes the money when I come out. I stopped going.' The second woman nods without speaking.

At the water point, a community health worker has been keeping a tally. Eleven children under five. Persistent coughs. The truck idling zone.

The donor review begins on Monday. The programme is suspended.

scenes.title: Prevention Pays
scenes.narrative_summary: Scale-up is seven days late. Carlos sends a two-word email to HQ: 'Brief delay.' In those seven days: the Google Drive folder is deleted and replaced with an encrypted, access-restricted database requiring individual login credentials. PSEA orientation runs with all 23 staff and volunteers — code of conduct signed, a reporting pathway posted at every distribution point in Kailani and Tevita dialect. The cash modality in Tevita Village is modified to allow women to collect in a private space without family members present. The truck fleet is rerouted 200 metres from the water point. 'We didn't expect you to actually move them,' a woman at the water point tells Sione at the next distribution, genuinely surprised. Three months into the programme, the protection cluster coordinator contacts Maya to request a copy of HopeForward's do-no-harm framework as a model for other agencies in the response.
scenes.learning_point_summary: Prevention takes time upfront but prevents exponentially greater harm and cost downstream. Addressing all five requirements simultaneously is achievable with one week of focused effort.
