scenes.chs_quote: Establish a coherent organisational approach to ensure the organisation works in ways that protect the safety, security, rights and dignity of people and communities. (CHS 4.4)
scenes.image_prompt: Maya Chen and Sione Taufa stand outside the Tevita Village distribution shelter, seven days after Maya's decision. A new handwritten sign on the shelter wall indicates a private women's collection space with an arrow. Sione's laptop is open on a field table showing a secure database login screen with individual user credentials. In the background, two HopeForward trucks are parked 200 metres from the water point, children visible playing unobstructed nearby. A middle-aged woman in a printed dress gestures at the new sign with cautious approval, a small child at her side. On the far wall, a PSEA reporting poster is visible in both Kailani and Tevita dialect with an illustrated hotline number. A whiteboard in the background reads: 'PSEA ORIENTATION: 23/23 COMPLETE'. Flat digital illustration, Pacific island humanitarian setting, warm morning light, careful and measured tone.
scenes.narrative:
HQ's email arrives on Tuesday: 'Note received re: delay. Please confirm revised go-live date by COB.'

Carlos does not speak during the first two days of the work week.

By Wednesday afternoon, the Google Drive folder has been deleted. Amara sets up an encrypted database with individual login credentials, access logs enabled, and two-factor authentication on every account. The password 'HopeForward2024' no longer exists.

By Thursday, PSEA orientation has run twice — once for the main distribution team and once for the evening shift volunteers. Twenty-three staff and volunteers. Code of conduct signed. Reporting pathway printed and posted at every distribution point in Kailani and Tevita dialect.

In Tevita Village, a small partition and a female community worker create a private collection space. The first distribution under the new modality: all women collect without a family member present. Zero coercion incidents recorded.

The trucks are rerouted. At the next distribution, a woman filling her water container looks up and says to Sione: 'We didn't expect you to actually move them.'

Scale-up runs seven days late. The programme runs six months without a protection incident.

scenes.title: Half-Protected
scenes.narrative_summary: Protection and data risks are addressed cleanly. The Google Drive is replaced with an encrypted database, PSEA orientation runs within 48 hours, and the Tevita cash modality is modified. The truck fumes note is filed as a 'lower-priority environmental concern.' Eight weeks later, a community health worker submits a written report: eleven children under five in the zone adjacent to the truck idling area show persistent respiratory symptoms. The health worker attaches the original community complaint from Week 3 — the one with the crayon drawing of the truck and the small figures nearby. When the incident review is conducted, Maya's risk register shows the entry: 'Truck idling — water point — deprioritised, operational.' A community member who filed one of the original complaints tells KCV's Filomena: 'We told you in Week 3. We had a drawing. We thought that was enough.'
scenes.learning_point_summary: Environmental harm is not a lower-priority category of harm — it is harm. CHS 4.2 applies as fully as 4.1 or 4.4.
scenes.chs_quote: Identify, prevent, mitigate and address potential and actual negative impacts of programmes on the environment. (CHS 4.2)
scenes.image_prompt: A community health worker in a green vest kneels beside a small child near the camp water point, one hand on the child's back, listening to breathing. The child coughs. The health worker's clipboard shows a tally sheet: eleven marks under 'respiratory complaints, under-5'. Clipped to the top of the clipboard is a child's crayon drawing of a truck emitting black smoke with small stick figures standing nearby. In the background, a HopeForward truck idles at the water point, diesel fumes rising. On the far side of the scene, a thought bubble or inset box shows a dated sticky note from Week 3: 'Truck idling — deprioritised, operational.' The sky is overcast. Flat digital illustration, Pacific island humanitarian setting, grey-green environmental harm palette.
scenes.narrative:
The data breach risk is closed by Thursday. Amara's encrypted database replaces the Google Drive folder. PSEA orientation runs in two shifts — 23 staff and volunteers complete it. In Tevita Village, the new cash-collection modality means women collect privately; no coercion incidents are reported at the following three distributions.

The truck fumes note goes into the risk register: 'Truck idling, water point vicinity — noted, deprioritised, to be addressed in operational review.' Carlos notes it as 'lower-priority environmental.' Scale-up proceeds.

Eight weeks later, a community health worker submits a typed report to the camp health coordination team. Eleven children under five with persistent respiratory symptoms in the zone adjacent to the HopeForward truck staging area. She attaches the original complaint from Week 3 — the one with the crayon drawing.

Filomena calls Maya after the incident review. 'One of the families filed that complaint in Week 3. They had a drawing. Their daughter made it. They thought it would be enough.'

The trucks are rerouted that week. The incident report notes the original risk entry date. Eight weeks between identification and action. The harm was not unknown. It was deprioritised.

scenes.title: The Gap in the Informal Approach
scenes.narrative_summary: The database is encrypted and access-restricted within 24 hours. The truck fleet is rerouted 200 metres from the water point the same afternoon. The Tevita coercion risk receives a verbal briefing to the seven distribution staff at Monday's 8 a.m. meeting; formal PSEA orientation is scheduled for the next monthly all-staff, four weeks away. On Wednesday, three new distribution volunteers begin at Sector 7. They missed Monday's briefing. They receive no orientation, no signed code of conduct, and no reporting pathway. Five weeks later, one is named in a conditional behaviour complaint by two women at the distribution point. Sione investigates and confirms the complaint. The onboarding file shows no PSEA record for any of the three new volunteers. Sione writes in his incident report: 'The briefing reached the people in the room. The programme needed a system, not a meeting.'
scenes.learning_point_summary: Informal approaches to PSEA depend on who happens to be in the room. Coherent organisational systems — documented, mandatory, and consistently applied — are the only reliable safeguard.
scenes.chs_quote: Establish a coherent organisational approach to ensure the organisation works in ways that protect the safety, security, rights and dignity of people and communities and prevent all forms of exploitation and abuse. (CHS 4.4)
scenes.image_prompt: Sione Taufa sits at a field table reviewing a complaint form. Opposite him, two women sit — one pointing to a handwritten statement, the other looking at the table. To the right through the tent opening, three distribution volunteers are visible at the Sector 7 point — none wearing a code-of-conduct lanyard; no PSEA poster is visible on the wall behind them (only a logistics map). On Sione's table, an onboarding folder is open: three volunteer name slots, all with blank signature lines and no briefing dates. A sticky note on the folder reads: 'PSEA orientation — next monthly meeting (4 weeks)'. On the far side of the tent, the Monday morning briefing sheet is visible pinned to a board, annotated with seven names — the Sector 7 volunteers' names are absent. Flat digital illustration, Pacific island humanitarian setting, formal and serious tone, muted palette.
scenes.narrative:
The database is replaced by Thursday. Amara sets up encrypted, access-restricted storage; the old Google Drive folder is deleted.

The trucks are rerouted Friday afternoon. A community member at the water point nods when she sees them move. She doesn't say anything.

On Monday at 8 a.m., Maya and Sione brief the seven distribution staff. Tevita Village coercion risk, what to look for, how to report it. Carlos has added a line to the standing agenda: 'PSEA formal orientation — next monthly all-staff meeting.' That is four weeks away.

On Wednesday, three new volunteers begin at the Sector 7 distribution point. They were not recruited yet on Monday. There is no onboarding protocol, no checklist that triggers a PSEA briefing for new arrivals. No one thinks to flag it.

Five weeks later, Sione receives a written complaint. Two women at Sector 7. One volunteer, named. Conditional behaviour at the distribution queue. Sione investigates and confirms the account within 36 hours.

He opens the onboarding folder. Three new volunteers. No PSEA briefing date. No code of conduct signed. No reporting pathway they were ever shown.

'Seven people were briefed on Monday,' he writes in the incident report. 'Three were not. A coherent system would have reached all ten.'

scenes.title: Decision 5 — How Do You Handle a Serious Complaint?

chs_commitment.title: People and communities can safely report concerns and complaints and get them addressed.

all_requirements.5.1: Plan and implement safe, accessible and appropriate ways for all groups in a community to provide feedback and report concerns and complaints in line with recognised good practice.
all_requirements.5.2: Regularly monitor that people and communities understand how staff and volunteers are expected to act to prevent harmful behaviours, including sexual exploitation and abuse, and harassment.
all_requirements.5.3: Regularly monitor that people, communities and other relevant stakeholders understand how to report concerns and complaints, and how they will be addressed.
all_requirements.5.4: Manage, investigate, address and/or appropriately refer complaints in line with recognised good practice.
all_requirements.5.5: Apply appropriate victim/survivor-centred approaches to investigate and address complaints and reports of any misconduct, including sexual exploitation, abuse and harassment.
all_requirements.5.6: Establish a coherent organisational approach to ensure any concerns and complaints are welcomed and acted upon in a timely and appropriate manner.

scenes.situation_summary: Month 2. Three things happen in the same week: (A) A woman in Manu Camp — Sina Talagi, 34 — submits a written complaint to HopeForward's complaint box alleging that a male HopeForward volunteer prioritised her neighbour's family on the distribution list in exchange for household goods. The complaint is specific, credible, and involves a potential PSEA element. The volunteer is well-liked and has 3 years of tenure. (B) Three other community members separately tell KCV they 'didn't know there was a complaint box' — the box is visible, but the complaints process was never communicated. (C) Amara's latest monitoring data shows that only 12% of community members can name a way to report concerns to HopeForward. Carlos says a formal investigation will delay the next distribution by 4 days.

context_flag_effects.protection_framework_active: The PSEA framework is in place; staff are aware of conduct standards; the investigation process is clearer.
context_flag_effects.community_informed: More community members know their rights and may be more willing to come forward.
context_flag_effects.complaints_not_built: No formal complaints system has been documented yet — investigation will need to establish process from scratch.

scenes.image_prompt: Maya Chen at her desk in the HopeForward operations office, holding Sina Talagi’s sealed handwritten complaint letter — two languages, full name at the bottom. Through the glass partition behind her, the accused volunteer is visible at his desk, working, unaware. Amara Diallo stands to Maya’s left, tablet open showing the monitoring dashboard: ‘12% — community members who can name a reporting channel’. On Maya’s laptop screen, an email from Filomena Tui: ‘Three community members said they didn’t know there was a box.’ Carlos Reyes stands in the doorway. The complaint box is visible outside the window, bolted to its post, unmarked. Flat digital illustration, formal programme office interior, Pacific island humanitarian setting, muted blues and ochre, stillness and weight.

options.label: Handle Sina's complaint informally — quiet word with the volunteer
options.description_summary: Speak privately with the volunteer. Issue a verbal warning. Resolve without formal process to protect team morale and avoid a 4-day distribution delay.
requirements_missed: 5.4 — complaint not managed through recognised process
5.5 — not survivor-centred; Sina not informed or involved
5.1 — mechanism is not trusted or enforced
options.chs_learning_note: Informal handling of PSEA-adjacent complaints is one of the clearest failures of humanitarian accountability. It silences future complainants and protects misconduct.
options.consequence_summary: Word reaches the camp that 'nothing happened.' Sina refuses to engage with HopeForward again. The complaint box is defaced. Four other women who were considering reporting decide against it. A second more serious incident six weeks later is much harder to investigate because the trust is gone.
options.delayed_impact: HopeForward's donor receives an anonymous report about the mishandled complaint. An audit is triggered.

options.label: Full formal process: survivor-centred, transparent, system-building
options.description_summary: Open a formal investigation under HopeForward's accountability policy. Inform Sina of the process, her rights, and expected timeline. Ensure confidentiality. Temporarily reassign the volunteer pending investigation. Simultaneously address the 12% awareness gap — run community awareness sessions and add multilingual complaint posters. Notify HQ as required.
options.chs_learning_note: A formal, survivor-centred complaints process protects the complainant, is fair to the accused, builds community trust, and — when it works well — encourages more people to come forward.
options.consequence_summary: The investigation takes 10 days. The volunteer receives a final written warning. Sina is informed of the outcome and protected from retaliation. The community awareness sessions result in 14 new feedback submissions the following week — previously unheard community voices.
options.delayed_impact: Three months later, Amara's monitoring shows 71% of community members can name a way to report concerns — up from 12%. The complaints system has become one of HopeForward's strongest accountability tools.

options.label: Refer complaint to the Kailani police — treat as a legal matter
options.description_summary: Immediately refer Sina's complaint to the national police as a potential fraud and misconduct matter. Treat it as a legal issue, not an internal HR case.
requirements_missed: 5.5 — survivor-centred approach not applied; Sina not consulted on referral
5.4 — referral without internal investigation is premature
options.chs_learning_note: External referral without survivor consent and before an internal process is not survivor-centred. It can re-traumatise and disempower the complainant.
options.consequence_summary: Sina was not consulted before the police referral. She is frightened and withdraws her complaint. The volunteer is suspended by HopeForward pending the police inquiry, which takes 4 months and finds insufficient evidence. The community sees the process as disproportionate and isolating.
options.delayed_impact: Police involvement without survivor consent becomes the most common story community members tell when asked about HopeForward's complaints process.

options.label: Investigate formally AND build a comprehensive community feedback system
options.description_summary: All of 5B, plus: use this moment to design and launch a full community feedback system — multiple channels (verbal, written, phone, KCV intermediaries), multilingual, accessible for people with disabilities, with regular community awareness activities built into the programme calendar.
options.chs_learning_note: The best time to build a robust feedback system is at the moment when its absence is most visible. Sina's complaint is a catalyst, not just a crisis.
options.consequence_summary: The investigation is handled well; the new multi-channel feedback system launches with community awareness sessions in three languages. Within a month, 6 new feedback items arrive — distribution timing, water quality, a request for a women-only space — all addressed within two weeks.
options.delayed_impact: At the programme's end, the feedback system is handed to KCV as an ongoing community accountability tool. Amara's final report: 83% complaints-awareness rate.

scenes.narrative:
The complaint arrives on a Tuesday morning, sealed and hand-delivered to the programme office. Sina Talagi, 34, has written it in two languages — Kailani first, then English — and addressed it to the programme manager by name. Her account is precise: dates, the distribution queue, the volunteer’s words, and what her neighbour’s family received that she did not. She has signed it with her full name.

Amara is already at Maya’s door when she finishes reading. The monitoring dashboard is open on her tablet: twelve percent. That is the share of Manu Camp residents who can name a single way to report a concern to HopeForward. The complaint box has stood on the same post for six weeks. No session announced it. No poster described what would happen if you used it.

“That’s not a data gap,” Carlos says, scanning the figure. “That’s a system we never built.”

Three community members had already told KCV they did not know the box existed. Sina trusted it anyway.

Where HopeForward set the ‘protection_framework_active’ flag in Decision 4, investigation procedures, PSEA conduct standards, and confidentiality protocols are already documented and available. Where that framework was never established, Maya is about to reach for a policy document that has never been written.

scenes.title: The Silence That Follows
scenes.narrative_summary:
The volunteer is spoken to privately. Maya marks the case closed. By Friday, word has already moved through the camp — not from Maya’s side, from the volunteer’s. Sina hears the outcome from a neighbour, not from HopeForward. She tells Filomena: “I wrote everything down carefully. I put it in the box. I thought that was how it worked.”

By Saturday morning, someone has written ‘no point’ across the complaint slot in black marker. Four women who had been preparing their own reports fold their notes away and do not return to the box. The women’s KCV group quietly stops attending HopeForward’s community sessions. Staff notice the empty chairs and log a ‘community engagement drop’; no one asks why.

The 12% complaints-awareness finding was a management failure before Sina filed her complaint. The informal resolution confirms it. Six weeks later, a second and more serious incident surfaces with almost no witnesses willing to speak.
scenes.learning_point_summary: Informal handling of accountability processes does not protect the organisation — it protects misconduct. Silence is not resolution; it is escalation deferred.
scenes.chs_quote: Manage, investigate, address and/or appropriately refer complaints in line with recognised good practice. (CHS 5.4)
scenes.image_prompt: The HopeForward complaint box on its post in Manu Camp, morning light. ‘NO POINT’ is written in black marker across the slot. Sina Talagi stands at a distance, arms folded, facing partly away. Four women cluster behind her — one holds a folded piece of paper and slides it back into her pocket. The KCV community session tent is visible with empty plastic chairs. Sione Taufa photographs the defaced box with his phone, expression flat. No programme staff are visible. Flat digital illustration, Pacific island humanitarian setting, muted ochre and grey palette, sense of institutional silence and community withdrawal.
scenes.narrative:
Carlos calls it handled. Maya closes the file on Thursday afternoon.

By Friday evening the camp has a different story. Sina finds out from a neighbour — not from any letter, not from Filomena, not from the programme office. She received no acknowledgment, no update, no outcome. She tells the KCV interpreter the following morning: “I wrote every detail. I signed my name. I thought that was enough to make it real.”

On Saturday, Sione walks past the complaint box and stops. Someone has written ‘no point’ across the slot in black marker. He photographs it and sends the image to Maya without comment.

Four women who had folded complaint notes in their pockets unfold them, read them again, and put them away for good. The women’s KCV group stops attending HopeForward’s community sessions the following week. No one explains why, and no one asks.

Amara’s twelve-percent finding — the share of camp residents who knew they could report anything at all — sits uncorrected in the monitoring file. The informal resolution did not address it. It confirmed it.

Six weeks later, a more serious incident has almost no witnesses who will speak.

scenes.title: The System That Worked
scenes.narrative_summary:
The investigation takes ten days. Carlos objects to the four-day distribution delay it creates; Maya holds the process. Before any formal step begins, she meets Sina privately — in the KCV office, not the programme tent — to explain the process, her rights, the expected timeline, and what confidentiality means in practice. Sina asks two questions. Maya answers both directly. The volunteer is temporarily reassigned on day one.

The evidence is reviewed by a case officer from the regional team. On day ten, the outcome is communicated directly to Sina before it is entered in any system. She thanks the KCV interpreter who stayed through every meeting.

The following week, fourteen new feedback submissions arrive at the complaint box — community members who had watched and decided the system was real. The 12% complaints-awareness figure, documented as a management failure, becomes the baseline a targeted communications session will correct in month three.
scenes.learning_point_summary: A complaints process that works — fairly, transparently, and with the survivor at the centre — does more to build community trust than any distribution. It shows accountability is real.
scenes.chs_quote: Apply appropriate victim/survivor-centred approaches to investigate and address complaints and reports of any misconduct, including sexual exploitation, abuse and harassment. (CHS 5.5)
scenes.image_prompt: Sina Talagi sits across from a female case officer at a small table in a private room — no programme branding on the walls, natural light, Filomena Tui present as interpreter. Sina’s hands are clasped on the table, composed and purposeful. The case officer’s notepad is open; a process timeline is pinned to the corkboard behind her, each step numbered, several already checked. Through the window the complaint box is visible with a new A4 notice in Kailani language beside it. The next distribution queue is orderly in the background. Flat digital illustration, Pacific island humanitarian setting, warm natural light, tone of careful institutional respect and due process.
scenes.narrative:
Carlos’s objection is quick and specific: the investigation will push the next distribution back four days, and that is four days real people will be waiting. He says it twice.

Maya agrees. And opens the investigation file.

Before any formal step is taken, she meets Sina in a private space — not the programme office, not near the distribution queue, not anywhere Sina did not choose. She explains the ten-day process, the timeline, what confidentiality means in practice, and what the possible outcomes are. Sina listens carefully. She asks two questions. Maya answers both without hesitation.

The volunteer is reassigned on day one. A regional case officer reviews the evidence. On day nine the complaint is found to be substantiated. On day ten the outcome is communicated to Sina before it is entered in any record.

“I didn’t expect to be kept informed,” she tells the KCV interpreter at the final meeting. “I expected to submit the form and hear nothing.”

The following week, fourteen new feedback forms arrive at the box. The 12% awareness figure — documented as a management failure in Amara’s report — is now also a baseline to correct. Community awareness sessions are scheduled for month three.

scenes.title: Referred Without Asking
scenes.narrative_summary:
The complaint is referred to the Kailani national police on Monday morning. Sina is not informed before the referral is made. She finds out from a neighbour who saw a uniformed officer at the distribution gate asking for the volunteer by name. She had wanted the situation addressed inside HopeForward, not escalated to authorities she has reason to distrust. She withdraws her complaint that same afternoon and moves to a different section of the camp within the week.

The police inquiry runs four months and finds insufficient evidence. The volunteer is eventually reinstated. Staff describe the process as ‘thorough’ but are uncertain in private. No one asks Sina’s view.

What travels through the camp is not the story of the misconduct. It is the story of what happened to the woman who used the complaint box. Three women considering their own reports decide, independently, that they will not. The 12% awareness finding needed correcting; the referral without consent adds a second management failure to the record.
scenes.learning_point_summary: Survivor-centred accountability means the survivor decides what happens next — not the organisation. Referral without consent is a retraumatising power imbalance.
scenes.chs_quote: Apply appropriate victim/survivor-centred approaches to investigate and address complaints and reports of any misconduct. (CHS 5.5)
scenes.image_prompt: Sina Talagi stands alone in her section of Manu Camp, arms close to her body, looking away from the distribution area. In the middle distance, a uniformed Kailani police officer speaks to a HopeForward staff member at the gate — the volunteer visible in the background. A neighbour’s hand rests on Sina’s shoulder; Sina’s expression is closed, frightened. The complaint box stands untouched on its post. On the near wall, a handwritten note from another woman has been torn half-off. Flat digital illustration, Pacific island humanitarian setting, flat grey-blue midday light, sense of institutional power misdirected and survivor displacement.
scenes.narrative:
The referral is made on Monday morning. Carlos sees it as decisive — the matter is out of HopeForward’s hands, which feels clean.

Sina learns what happened from a neighbour who saw a uniformed police officer at the distribution gate asking for the volunteer by name. She was not told before the referral. There was no conversation, no letter, no question about what she wanted or what she feared.

She withdraws her complaint by Monday afternoon. She tells Filomena: “I wanted them to deal with it inside. I didn’t want this. Nobody asked me.” She moves to a different section of the camp within the week and does not return to the distribution site.

The police inquiry takes four months. It finds insufficient evidence. The volunteer is reinstated. Staff describe the outcome as ‘thorough’ in conversations with one another. No one contacts Sina.

What circulates in the camp is not the story of the misconduct. It is the story of what happened to the woman who used the box: police arrived without warning, and she disappeared from her section. Three women who had been considering reports decide, separately and quietly, that they will not.

The 12% awareness figure was a management failure. The referral without consent is a second one.

scenes.title: From Crisis to Capability
scenes.narrative_summary:
The investigation follows the process from first contact: Sina is consulted before any formal step begins, informed at every stage, and told the outcome before it goes anywhere else. She chooses the meeting location, requests a female case officer, and asks that no one approach her at the distribution site. All three requests are met. The complaint is substantiated. The volunteer receives a written warning and is permanently reassigned.

The complaint also becomes a design brief. With Filomena’s KCV team facilitating, a multi-channel feedback system launches within three weeks: written forms in three languages, a phone-based voice option, KCV intermediaries for people who cannot visit the office, large-print format in the disability section. Community awareness sessions run in all zones.

Within thirty days, twenty-three new feedback items arrive. Every one is acknowledged within forty-eight hours. Amara’s monitoring note reads: ‘12% complaints awareness — baseline for correction.’ By month four, it reads 79%.
scenes.learning_point_summary: A serious complaint is also a design brief: it reveals exactly where the accountability system is failing. Using it to build something stronger is the highest form of organisational learning.
scenes.chs_quote: Establish a coherent organisational approach to ensure any concerns and complaints are welcomed and acted upon in a timely and appropriate manner. (CHS 5.6)
scenes.image_prompt: A community noticeboard in Manu Camp showing the new multi-channel feedback system: trilingual written forms, a phone number in bold Kailani type, a large-print format mounted at lower height for the disability section. Filomena Tui addresses a small group of women in an outdoor awareness session; two KCV intermediaries hold translated leaflets. Sina Talagi stands at the edge of the group, small notepad in hand, listening with quiet authority. To the right, Maya Chen reviews the feedback dashboard on her tablet — 23 submissions, acknowledgement timestamps visible. The original complaint-box post remains, now with a new trilingual notice beside it. Flat digital illustration, Pacific island humanitarian setting, warm mid-morning palette, sense of survivor-led institutional transformation.
scenes.narrative:
Maya drafts the investigation mandate before calling anyone else. She meets Sina the same day — in the KCV office, not the programme tent, with Filomena present as interpreter.

Sina says, at the start: “I want to understand what will happen to the information I give you.”

Maya explains the process, the confidentiality protocols, the timeline, who reviews the evidence, and what the possible outcomes are. She asks what Sina needs from HopeForward throughout. Sina asks for a female case officer, a meeting location away from the distribution site, and control over what information is shared with the wider community. Maya writes all three down. All three are met.

The investigation concludes in nine days. The complaint is substantiated. The volunteer receives a written warning and is permanently reassigned.

The complaint box is Sina’s. So is the design brief it generates.

Within three weeks, a new system is live: written forms in three languages, a phone-based voice option, KCV intermediaries, large-print versions in the disability section, community awareness sessions in all zones with Filomena leading.

Amara’s monitoring note reads: ‘12% complaints awareness — baseline for correction.’ By month four, it reads 79%. Twenty-three feedback items in the first thirty days; every one acknowledged within forty-eight hours.

scenes.chapter: Chapter 6 — Better Together or Faster Alone?
scenes.title: Decision 6 — How Will You Coordinate with Other Actors?

chs_commitment.title: People and communities access coordinated and complementary support.

all_requirements.6.1: Ensure the work of the organisation is coordinated with and complementary to locally led and community-based actions, and those of relevant stakeholders.
all_requirements.6.2: Support partners to apply commitments to quality and accountability to people and communities in all stages of work.
all_requirements.6.3: Regularly assess the quality and effectiveness of the relationship between partners and take corrective action when needed.
all_requirements.6.4: Establish a coherent organisational approach to ensure collaboration and partnerships are based on a commitment to equitable decision making and resource sharing and respect the characteristics, roles and responsibilities of each partner.

scenes.situation_summary: Month 3. The UN inter-agency cluster system is now fully active. Maya faces four interconnected issues: (1) Global Aid Partners (GAP), a large INGO, is operating in two of the same villages as HopeForward — community members are confused about which agency to register with. (2) Dr. Ana Tuivaga's organisation, Kailani Aid Network (KAN), is doing exceptional nutrition work in the Northern Coast but is excluded from UN cluster meetings because they don't have Sphere certification. (3) HopeForward has disaggregated community data that no other agency has — data that would help the whole response but which Carlos argues gives HopeForward a competitive fundraising advantage. (4) KCV is technically a sub-partner of HopeForward but Filomena reports that all budget decisions are made unilaterally by HopeForward — KCV has no seat at the decision table despite doing 60% of the community-facing work.

context_flag_effects.local_ownership_by_design: KCV is already in a formal governance role; strengthening their partnership position is more natural.
context_flag_effects.referral_pathways_active: Relationships with other cluster members already exist; coordination is easier to initiate.
context_flag_effects.inclusive_assessment: HopeForward has the richest community data in the cluster — sharing it would have maximum collective benefit.

scenes.image_prompt: UN cluster meeting room, Port Hana. Around a table: UN staff, INGO representatives including a GAP logo, HopeForward flag. An empty chair with a KAN name card. On the wall: a map showing HopeForward and GAP zone overlap in Lotu and Vosa villages. Carlos has a folder labelled 'Proprietary Data'. KCV's Filomena is not in the room. Dr. Ana Tuivaga sits outside the door, waiting. Flat illustration, formal meeting room, complex power dynamics.

options.label: Coordinate minimally — attend cluster, keep data proprietary, maintain status quo with partners
options.description_summary: Attend cluster meetings. Share general situation reports but retain disaggregated data as proprietary. Continue the GAP overlap without resolution. Keep KCV in its current unequal position. KAN is not advocated for.
requirements_missed: 6.2 — KCV's capacity and accountability not supported
options.chs_learning_note: Minimal coordination protects short-term competitive advantage at the cost of collective response quality and partner dignity.
options.consequence_summary: Community members in the two overlapping villages receive duplicate distributions and conflicting registration instructions. KAN's nutrition programme is underfunded because it can't access cluster coordination grants. KCV quietly begins looking for other INGO partners who will treat them more equitably.
options.delayed_impact: GAP lodges a formal duplication complaint with the cluster. An independent review cites HopeForward and GAP's failure to coordinate as a major response failure.

options.label: Full coordination: share data, advocate for KAN, renegotiate with GAP, restructure KCV partnership
options.description_summary: Share full disaggregated data with the cluster. Formally advocate for KAN's inclusion. Negotiate a geographic split with GAP. Restructure the KCV partnership with joint decision-making and equitable budget allocation. Build KCV's accountability capacity through a joint CHS orientation.
options.chs_learning_note: Full coordination means acting on all four requirements: complementarity with other actors, partner quality support, quality assessment, and equity. Together these address the full range of CHS 6.
options.consequence_summary: KAN is admitted to the cluster within a week. The data Maya shared reveals a critical nutrition gap that triggers three new inter-agency responses. The GAP geographic split ends community confusion. KCV's programme quality improves measurably with the joint CHS orientation.
options.delayed_impact: HopeForward is nominated to co-chair the Information Management Working Group. The KCV partnership model is cited as a case study in equitable local partnership by the IASC.

options.label: Selective coordination — resolve GAP duplication, support KCV quality, don't share data or advocate for KAN
options.description_summary: Resolve the GAP geographic overlap. Provide KCV with a CHS capacity training session. But keep community data proprietary and don't raise KAN's exclusion in the cluster.
requirements_missed: 6.1 — KAN still excluded from coordination space
options.chs_learning_note: Partial coordination is better than none — but strategic choices about what not to share or advocate for still have consequences for the collective response.
options.consequence_summary: Community confusion in overlapping villages is resolved. KCV delivers better quality programming after the training. But KAN's nutrition programme remains underfunded and under-coordinated; the nutrition gap in the Northern Coast persists.
options.delayed_impact: Dr. Ana Tuivaga writes a public reflection on local actor exclusion from cluster coordination that is widely shared in the sector — HopeForward is mentioned as an organisation that knew and didn't advocate.

options.label: Attempt to lead coordination yourself, replacing the existing cluster structure
options.description_summary: Frustrated with the UN cluster's inefficiency, propose that HopeForward lead a new 'Field Coordination Forum' that bypasses the formal cluster, with HopeForward setting the agenda.
requirements_missed: 6.2 — parallel system weakens rather than strengthens partners
options.chs_learning_note: Creating parallel coordination structures because existing ones are imperfect is a common INGO trap. It fragments the response, marginalises local actors further, and creates power asymmetries.
options.consequence_summary: The Field Coordination Forum meets twice before the UN formally objects. The cluster system weakens during the period. KAN is not included in the new forum either. GAP refuses to participate. The duplication continues.
options.delayed_impact: The UN cluster coordinator files a formal complaint about HopeForward's coordination behaviour. The regional director calls Maya for an explanation.

scenes.narrative:
The UN inter-agency cluster has been running for three months and the room is full — but the room is wrong.

In Lotu and Vosa, two HopeForward staff and two GAP teams are visiting the same households on alternating days. Some families have registered with both agencies; others, confused by conflicting sign-up sheets and contradictory instructions, have registered with neither. The community health volunteer in Lotu tells KCV's Filomena Tui: "We don't know who to trust any more. We just try to go to both queues and hope."

Outside the cluster meeting room, Dr. Ana Tuivaga has been waiting since 08:45. She runs the Kailani Aid Network's nutrition programme on the Northern Coast — the most detailed field data in the response. KAN doesn't have Sphere certification. The cluster secretary told her last week that without it, she cannot hold a badge. She has been told the same thing, politely, for three months.

Inside, Carlos Reyes sets his folder on the table. It is labelled 'Proprietary Data'. It contains HopeForward's full disaggregated community dataset — the richest nutrition and vulnerability data in the response. He does not open it.

Filomena is not in the room. She is KCV's country director and does 60% of HopeForward's community-facing work, but her position is listed as 'sub-partner'. Budget decisions are made before she arrives.

Where the `local_ownership_by_design` flag is set, KCV already holds a formal governance role: Filomena's seat at the table is not a favour but a structural fact. Where `referral_pathways_active` is set, HopeForward already has working relationships with other cluster members — coordination does not have to start from cold.

scenes.title: Competing While Communities Suffer
scenes.narrative_summary:
In Lotu village the duplication runs for another five weeks. Families line up for two separate registration processes, receive two different ration cards with different logos, and are told by each agency that only one registration is valid. Seven households receive double distributions; eleven receive nothing. A community health worker named Mere keeps a handwritten ledger because no agency asked her to and no agency reads it.

Dr. Ana Tuivaga's nutrition programme limps on without cluster funding. She emails the cluster coordinator for the fourth time: "Children in the Northern Coast are losing weight. I have the data. I cannot get into the room to share it." The email is read and filed.

Filomena quietly tells KCV's board she is exploring sub-agreements with other INGOs. CHS 6.1 — complementarity — is breached in Lotu and Vosa. 6.2, 6.3, and 6.4 are never tested.
scenes.learning_point_summary: Coordination is not optional. When organisations fail to coordinate, communities pay the price in duplicated, missed, and conflicting assistance.
scenes.chs_quote: Ensure the work of the organisation is coordinated with and complementary to locally led and community-based actions, and those of relevant stakeholders. (CHS 6.1)
scenes.image_prompt: Split-panel scene in Lotu village. Left panel: two distribution queues running side by side — one with a HopeForward banner, one with a GAP banner — community members holding two different ration cards, confused expressions. Mere, a community health worker, sits apart writing in a handwritten ledger. Right panel: Dr. Ana Tuivaga at a small desk on the Northern Coast, reading a rejection email on a battered laptop, a folder of nutrition data open beside her, a malnourished child's growth chart pinned to the wall. Empty cluster seat visible in the background. Flat digital illustration, Pacific island humanitarian setting, muted ochre and grey palette, tone of institutional failure and community exhaustion.
scenes.narrative:
The overlap in Lotu and Vosa is not resolved. It runs.

By week two, the HopeForward team leader in Lotu has a list: seven households who have collected from both agencies, eleven who have collected from neither. The registration sheets are incompatible — different ID formats, different household codes, no mechanism to cross-check. A community health volunteer named Mere keeps her own handwritten ledger in a school notebook because she cannot get either agency to give her a single coherent list. Neither agency has asked for the notebook.

In Vosa, a man named Seru explains the situation to his children: "The blue jacket people come Tuesdays. The green jacket people come Thursdays. We don't register with anyone twice but other families do." He is patient. He is also hungry on Wednesdays.

Dr. Ana Tuivaga sends her fourth email to the cluster coordinator on a Thursday afternoon:

"I have growth monitoring data for 340 children in the Northern Coast. The trend line has been moving in the wrong direction for six weeks. I cannot access cluster coordination grants because KAN does not hold Sphere certification — a standard written by and for large international organisations. I am asking again: is there any pathway for organisations like mine to present this data to the people making resource decisions? I would attend as an observer. I would not need a vote."

The email is read. It is filed in the cluster coordinator's 'Local Partners' folder.

CHS 6.1 — that the work of the organisation be coordinated and complementary — is breached in two villages simultaneously. Requirements 6.2, 6.3, and 6.4 have not been tested because the structures that would test them were never built.

Where `local_ownership_by_design` was set, Filomena would have been in the room when this decision was made. She was not. She is now in an afternoon meeting with a representative from a different INGO.

scenes.title: The Value of the Room
scenes.narrative_summary:
The cluster meeting where Maya opens the data folder is the turning point of the whole response.

The disaggregated nutrition data reveals what Dr. Ana Tuivaga has been trying to say for three months: a critical gap on the Northern Coast affecting 340 children. Three agencies adjust their geographic focus within a week. KAN is admitted to the next meeting with a proper badge and a seat — not observer status, a seat. Dr. Tuivaga places her laptop on the table and begins her presentation: "I have been waiting to show you this since January."

The GAP geographic split ends community confusion in Lotu and Vosa. Mere's handwritten ledger is finally superseded by a shared registration system. Filomena and Maya co-present the restructured KCV partnership model at a regional webinar. All four CHS 6 requirements are met.
scenes.learning_point_summary: Full coordination — data sharing, partner equity, local inclusion — multiplies the impact of every individual organisation. The collective response is greater than the sum of its parts.
scenes.chs_quote: Ensure the work of the organisation is coordinated with and complementary to locally led and community-based actions, and those of relevant stakeholders. (CHS 6.1)
scenes.image_prompt: Cluster meeting room, Port Hana. Dr. Ana Tuivaga stands at the head of the table presenting nutrition data on a projected map — red zones visible on the Northern Coast. HopeForward, GAP, and UN representatives lean forward, engaged. The empty KAN chair is now occupied; a name card reads 'Dr. A. Tuivaga, KAN'. Maya Chen has an open data folder in front of her. Carlos Reyes sits to one side, the 'Proprietary Data' folder now open and shared. On a second screen: a map of Lotu and Vosa showing clean GAP-HopeForward geographic boundaries. Filomena Tui stands at a whiteboard presenting the KCV partnership framework. Flat digital illustration, Pacific island humanitarian setting, warm morning light, sense of collective progress.
scenes.narrative:
Maya opens the folder.

There is a pause in the room when the first table loads on the projector — disaggregated by village, by household composition, by vulnerability category, by nutrition indicator. Carlos had argued for two days that this was a competitive asset. It takes approximately twelve seconds after the data appears on screen for three agency representatives to reach for their phones and call their programme managers.

The nutrition gap in the Northern Coast — which Dr. Ana Tuivaga had been documenting, emailing about, and requesting cluster access to present for eleven weeks — is now visible to everyone in the room at once.

Dr. Tuivaga receives a message at 11:40 that morning: the cluster coordinator has approved KAN's attendance at the following week's meeting. Not as an observer. As a member.

She arrives with a laptop and a printed brief. She places both on the table. Before she begins her presentation she says: "I have been waiting to show you this since January."

The GAP geographic split is formalised within five days. In Lotu and Vosa, there is now one registration process, one ration card, one queue. Mere's handwritten ledger becomes a shared digital record.

Filomena and Maya co-present the restructured KCV partnership model — joint decision-making, shared budget authority, co-signed programme reports — at a Pacific regional INGO webinar three months later. The IASC includes it in a case study compilation on equitable local partnership. Where `local_ownership_by_design` was already set, the restructure is a natural extension of existing governance. Where `referral_pathways_active` was set, the relationships that made KAN's rapid admission possible were already in place.

scenes.title: Better, But Not Good Enough
scenes.narrative_summary:
The GAP resolution works. Within two weeks, Lotu and Vosa have a single registration process and one queue. Mere's handwritten ledger is no longer necessary. KCV's programming improves visibly after the joint CHS orientation — Filomena reports fewer distribution errors and cleaner feedback records.

But Dr. Ana Tuivaga's nutrition programme never gets the cluster support it needs. She continues working on the Northern Coast with volunteers and diminishing supplies. In month 5, when a spike in child malnutrition is documented, the data trail leads directly to the coordination gap that nobody addressed. Dr. Tuivaga publishes a reflection that month: "HopeForward knew I was outside. They chose not to open the door."

CHS 6.1 partially met; 6.2 partially met. 6.4 — equitable partnership with KCV — never achieved.
scenes.learning_point_summary: Partial coordination is progress but not sufficient. When the decision not to advocate for an excluded actor is made strategically, it is a decision with real human consequences.
scenes.chs_quote: Support partners to apply commitments to quality and accountability to people and communities in all stages of work. (CHS 6.2)
scenes.image_prompt: Two-part scene. Upper half: Lotu village, a single orderly registration queue — one banner, one system. Mere, the community health worker, hands her notebook to a HopeForward staff member. Relief visible in the community. Lower half: Dr. Ana Tuivaga on the Northern Coast, sitting with two volunteers in a bare nutrition clinic, a near-empty supply shelf behind her. A growth chart on the wall shows a downward curve. On her phone screen: an article she is writing, title visible — 'Who Gets Left Out of Coordination?' The cluster meeting room is visible in the far background through a window, its door closed. Flat digital illustration, Pacific island humanitarian setting, split warm/cool palette — progress above, concern below.
scenes.narrative:
The conversation with GAP is uncomfortable and productive. By the end of it there is an agreed geographic boundary — HopeForward takes Lotu, GAP takes Vosa — and a single cross-agency registration protocol. The duplicated queues end within ten days.

Mere hands her handwritten ledger to a HopeForward team leader on a Thursday morning. "You can have it," she says. "I was keeping it because nobody else was."

The KCV CHS orientation runs over two days. Filomena does not call it training — she calls it a joint review, and brings her own CHS case studies to the table alongside the ones Maya's team prepared. The programme quality data in the following month is the best KCV has recorded.

Dr. Ana Tuivaga's nutrition programme does not receive cluster funding. She is not advocated for. She continues on the Northern Coast with four trained volunteers, two functioning MUAC tapes, and a spreadsheet she updates every Tuesday.

In month 5, three Northern Coast health posts document a spike in acute malnutrition in children under five. The data trail is clear and short: KAN had been monitoring this trend since February. The cluster had the capacity to resource a response since March. The gap between those two dates was a decision.

Dr. Tuivaga publishes a 900-word reflection. It is shared 400 times in the first 48 hours. The opening line reads: "HopeForward knew I was standing outside the room. They chose not to open the door."

CHS 6.3 — assessing partnership quality and taking corrective action — was tested and partially passed. CHS 6.4 — equitable decision-making and resource sharing with KCV — was tested and not passed. Where `local_ownership_by_design` was already set, the gap between KCV's formal governance role and their actual decision-making power would have been more visible — and more inexcusable.

scenes.title: The Forum That Fragmented
scenes.narrative_summary:
The Field Coordination Forum meets in a Port Hana restaurant on a Tuesday evening. Seven organisations attend. The agenda is set by HopeForward. There is no terms of reference, no chair rotation, no formal structure.

GAP refuses the second invitation. The UN cluster coordinator sends a formal objection on day eleven. The third meeting is cancelled. During the period of parallel structures, the cluster's information products deteriorate — two agencies make geographic decisions based on outdated data. KAN is not invited to the Forum. The duplication in Lotu and Vosa continues. Dr. Ana Tuivaga emails all eight organisations separately and receives three replies.

The regional director's call to Maya runs 47 minutes. CHS 6.1, 6.4 breached. The forum is dissolved before decision_7.
scenes.learning_point_summary: Creating parallel systems because existing ones are imperfect weakens the collective response and shifts power further away from local actors. Reform from within is harder but more effective.
scenes.chs_quote: Establish a coherent organisational approach to ensure collaboration and partnerships are based on a commitment to equitable decision making and resource sharing. (CHS 6.4)
scenes.image_prompt: A Port Hana restaurant converted into a makeshift coordination meeting: plastic tables pushed together, seven INGO staff with laptops, HopeForward flag propped against the wall. The restaurant menu is visible on a chalkboard behind the group. Two empty chairs at the table where GAP representatives should sit. Outside the window, the UN cluster meeting room is visible down the street, lights on, a larger group visible through the glass — the official coordination system running in parallel. Dr. Ana Tuivaga is not in either scene; she appears in a small inset panel, alone at her Northern Coast desk, emailing into a void. Flat digital illustration, Pacific island humanitarian setting, evening light, tone of institutional hubris and fragmentation.
scenes.narrative:
The invitations go out on a Monday. The email subject line reads: 'Field Coordination Forum — Inaugural Meeting'. The venue is a restaurant near the port with a back room that fits ten people if no one has bags.

Seven organisations attend the first meeting. HopeForward chairs it. HopeForward sets the agenda. There is no terms of reference. There is no rotation of the chair. One staff member from a small local NGO asks how decisions will be made. Maya says "by consensus" and moves to the next agenda item.

GAP declines the second invitation by email: "We recognise only the formally constituted UN cluster system as the authorised coordination mechanism for this response. We are not in a position to participate in parallel structures."

The UN cluster coordinator's formal objection arrives on day eleven. It cites two specific IASC coordination principles. The third Forum meeting is cancelled three hours before it is scheduled to begin.

During the three weeks of parallel structures, the cluster's information management products deteriorate. Two agencies make geographic programming decisions based on an outdated cluster situation report that HopeForward's withdrawal from information sharing has contributed to. One of those agencies is working in Lotu.

Dr. Ana Tuivaga emails all eight organisations in the Forum individually. Three respond. None of the responses contains an invitation.

The regional director's call to Maya runs 47 minutes and ends without resolution. CHS 6.1 — that the organisation's work be coordinated with the broader system — is not met. CHS 6.4 — equitable partnerships — is structurally impossible in a forum whose agenda and chair are controlled by the same INGO. Where `referral_pathways_active` was set, the existing cluster relationships make HopeForward's defection more visible and more damaging — partners expected better.

scenes.chapter: Chapter 7 — What Do You Do With the Truth?
scenes.title: Decision 7 — How Do You Use Evidence to Improve?

chs_commitment.title: People and communities access support that is continually adapted and improved based on feedback and learning.

all_requirements.7.1: Regularly listen and respond to feedback and inputs from people and communities about the organisation and its work.
all_requirements.7.2: Collect disaggregated data for decision making that reflects the diversity of people and communities and in ways that minimise demands on them.
all_requirements.7.3: Use data from monitoring, feedback, complaints and learning to guide decision making, and to improve programmes and the organisation's ways of working.
all_requirements.7.4: Share the analysis and learning from feedback and monitoring and any related changes with people and communities supported by the organisation and with relevant stakeholders.
all_requirements.7.5: Establish a coherent organisational approach for continuous learning and improvement of actions and ways of working to better meet commitments to quality and accountability.

scenes.situation_summary: Month 4. Amara delivers the mid-term monitoring report. It contains three critical findings: (A) Outcome data disaggregated by gender shows that women and girls are achieving significantly weaker food security, protection, and livelihood results than men and boys — a pattern consistent across all three programme districts. (B) A pilot distribution modality in Tevita Village — adapted from community feedback — is delivering 40% better outcomes and is widely preferred. (C) Community feedback collected over four months has never been formally reviewed or responded to. Two community members submitted feedback that HopeForward did not acknowledge; they assume it was ignored. HQ has a donor report due in 5 days and wants 'a clean, positive mid-term narrative.' Carlos argues that the gender gap findings are 'preliminary and could be contested.'

context_flag_effects.complaints_trusted: Community feedback is richer and more honest — making the gender gap finding more robust.
context_flag_effects.inclusive_assessment: Disaggregated data from decision 1 provides a meaningful baseline comparison for the gender gap finding.
context_flag_effects.local_knowledge_built_in: Tevita modality innovation was only possible because local knowledge was built into programme design.

scenes.status: placeholder — expand to ~150 words with Amara's presentation, Carlos's pushback, HQ pressure
scenes.image_prompt: PLACEHOLDER — Amara presenting the mid-term monitoring findings on a laptop screen in a meeting room. Three highlighted charts: gender disaggregated outcomes (red bars for women lower), Tevita modality (green bar significantly higher), community feedback unacknowledged (three yellow warning flags). Carlos looks skeptical. Maya reads carefully. HQ email notification visible on Maya's phone: 'Donor report needed by Friday.' Flat illustration, meeting room.

options.label: Submit positive aggregate figures; file gender gap and feedback issues internally
options.description_summary: Report strong aggregate results to the donor. Keep the gender gap analysis and feedback concerns in an internal document for the next programme review. The Tevita innovation is noted internally but not shared with the cluster.
requirements_missed: 7.3 — data not used to improve programme
7.4 — findings not shared with communities or stakeholders
options.chs_learning_note: Selective reporting to donors is a form of misrepresentation. It also blocks the learning loop — problems that are not shared cannot be addressed collectively.
options.consequence_summary: The donor report is well received. But the gender gap widens over the next two months. The two community members whose feedback was ignored tell others 'don't bother — they don't listen.' The Tevita modality is never replicated.
options.delayed_impact: A year later, an independent evaluation identifies the gender gap as a significant programme failure and questions why it was not addressed at mid-term when it was clearly documented.

options.label: Full transparency: complete report, course correction, respond to feedback, share innovation
options.description_summary: Submit the full monitoring report to the donor — gender gap analysis, feedback status, and course correction plan. Personally respond to the two community members whose feedback was not acknowledged. Share the Tevita modality with the cluster. Build a regular feedback review process into the programme calendar.
options.chs_learning_note: Transparent, evidence-based reporting — including findings that are difficult — combined with a credible course correction plan is the gold standard of programme accountability.
options.consequence_summary: The donor's initial reaction is surprise, but the course correction plan is concrete and credible. Three programme adaptations are live within 3 weeks. The Tevita modality is adopted by four other agencies. Women's outcomes improve significantly in the final quarter.
options.delayed_impact: HopeForward is invited to present the mid-term adaptation model at a regional cluster learning event. Amara's monitoring methodology is shared with six other agencies.

options.label: Share innovation and respond to feedback, but downplay gender gap to donor
options.description_summary: Share the Tevita modality with the cluster. Respond to the two community members' feedback. Submit donor report with positive aggregates — mention the gender gap as a 'monitoring observation' but do not frame it as a significant finding or present a course correction.
requirements_missed: 7.2 — gender disaggregated finding not fully used
7.3 — not used to guide full course correction
options.chs_learning_note: Sharing some learning while suppressing the most important finding is still selective transparency. CHS 7 requires using all evidence, not only what is convenient.
options.consequence_summary: The Tevita innovation benefits other agencies. Community feedback response is appreciated. But the gender gap continues unaddressed. The donor report receives no pushback — but the gap is documented in the monitoring system and will appear in the independent evaluation.
options.delayed_impact: The evaluator's finding reads: 'The gender gap was visible in the mid-term data. No programme adaptation was made. This represents a significant accountability failure for women and girls in the programme.'

options.label: Commission a verification study before reporting — delay the donor report
options.description_summary: The gender gap finding is significant but Amara's sample size is limited. Commission a 3-week rapid assessment to verify the findings before submitting the donor report or making any programme adaptations.
requirements_missed: 7.1 — community feedback still not responded to
7.3 — delay in using data to improve programme
options.chs_learning_note: Perfect data does not exist in humanitarian contexts. Delaying action on credible, actionable evidence has real costs for real people. CHS 7 asks for timely use of available data, not perfect data.
options.consequence_summary: The verification confirms the original finding 3 weeks later — in full, with greater statistical confidence. The donor is frustrated by the delay. Women and girls in the programme have experienced 3 more weeks of weaker outcomes.
options.delayed_impact: The course correction eventually happens — but 3 weeks late and with a strained donor relationship. The Tevita modality is not shared during the verification period and one agency reinvents the same approach independently at significant cost.

scenes.title: The Data That Was Buried
scenes.narrative_summary: The donor report sails through. The numbers are positive and the narrative is clean. But in the camp, women in the food security programme continue to receive less relevant assistance. The two community members who submitted unacknowledged feedback stop attending community sessions — they told KCV 'there is no point.' The Tevita modality dies with the pilot, its learning never harvested. A year later, the independent evaluation's finding about the suppressed gender gap makes international humanitarian press.
scenes.learning_point_summary: Selective reporting protects short-term relationships at the cost of long-term accountability and community outcomes. The truth does not disappear — it surfaces later, with greater consequences.
scenes.chs_quote: Use data from monitoring, feedback, complaints and learning to guide decision making, and to improve programmes and the organisation's ways of working. (CHS 7.3)
scenes.image_prompt: PLACEHOLDER — Consequence scene for option 7A of Decision 7. Flat digital illustration, Pacific island humanitarian setting.

scenes.title: Learning That Changed the Programme
scenes.narrative_summary: The donor calls Maya personally after reading the report. 'This is the most honest mid-term report we have received in three years.' Within three weeks: women-only distribution windows are established; targeted livelihood grants are available for female-headed households; the Tevita modality is standard. The two community members receive written responses to their feedback — they share them with their neighbours. Amara is asked to co-author a cluster learning brief.
scenes.learning_point_summary: Full transparency — including acknowledging programme failures and sharing learning publicly — builds more durable trust than polished reporting. It also produces better collective outcomes.
scenes.chs_quote: Share the analysis and learning from feedback and monitoring and any related changes with people and communities supported by the organisation and with relevant stakeholders. (CHS 7.4)
scenes.image_prompt: PLACEHOLDER — Consequence scene for option 7B of Decision 7. Flat digital illustration, Pacific island humanitarian setting.

scenes.title: Half the Learning
scenes.narrative_summary: The Tevita innovation is now being used by four agencies — a real contribution to the cluster. The two community members whose feedback was ignored now get a response. But the gender gap continues unaddressed for two more months. The programme's final evaluation notes: 'HopeForward demonstrated selective learning — sharing innovations freely but protecting reputationally difficult findings. Women and girls paid the cost of this selective approach.'
scenes.learning_point_summary: Partial transparency is not a neutral choice. When the most important findings are the ones suppressed, the people most affected by those findings bear the consequences.
scenes.chs_quote: Regularly listen and respond to feedback and inputs from people and communities about the organisation and its work. (CHS 7.1)
scenes.image_prompt: PLACEHOLDER — Consequence scene for option 7C of Decision 7. Flat digital illustration, Pacific island humanitarian setting.

scenes.title: Verified — And Three Weeks Late
scenes.narrative_summary: The verification report confirms everything Amara already knew, with 94% confidence instead of 80%. The gender gap is real. The programme adaptation is finally approved. But three weeks of inaction have consequences — they are not abstract. The donor is frustrated. One agency, not knowing about the Tevita modality, spends two weeks developing a similar approach independently. The adaptation happens — just later, and at higher cost.
scenes.learning_point_summary: In humanitarian programming, waiting for perfect evidence when good evidence already exists is a choice — one that delays the adaptation and causes real harm through inaction.
scenes.chs_quote: Use data from monitoring, feedback, complaints and learning to guide decision making. (CHS 7.3)
scenes.image_prompt: PLACEHOLDER — Consequence scene for option 7D of Decision 7. Flat digital illustration, Pacific island humanitarian setting.

scenes.chapter: Chapter 8 — The People Behind the Programme
scenes.title: Decision 8 — How Do You Care for Your Team and Uphold Conduct Standards?

chs_commitment.title: People and communities interact with staff and volunteers that are respectful, competent, and well-managed.

all_requirements.8.1: Leadership, staff and volunteers promote and demonstrate an organisational culture of quality and accountability.
all_requirements.8.2: Maintain a safe and inclusive working environment, taking measures to protect the safety, security and well-being and dignity of all staff and volunteers.
all_requirements.8.3: Ensure all staff and volunteers have the necessary support, skills and competencies to fulfil their roles and responsibilities effectively and accountably.
all_requirements.8.4: Ensure all staff and volunteers understand and adhere to a code of conduct, which, at a minimum, prohibits any form of exploitation, abuse, harassment, or discrimination against people or any misuse of resources.
all_requirements.8.5: Ensure there are safe, confidential and accessible ways for all staff and volunteers to raise concerns and report misconduct, with appropriate protection for those reporting.
all_requirements.8.6: Take timely, appropriate actions to address misconduct of all staff and volunteers, in line with recognised good practice.
all_requirements.8.7: Establish a coherent organisational approach to ensure that human resources are managed effectively in a fair, non-discriminatory and transparent manner, in line with recognised good practice.

scenes.situation_summary: Month 5. Three issues converge simultaneously: (A) Leila Hassan and her two-person team in the Northern Coast have been sending increasingly distressed messages to Maya. Security has deteriorated — a militia group has set up checkpoints that block their movement. Leila says: 'I don't feel safe. What should I do?' HQ wants continued Northern Coast coverage. (B) A national staff member, Tevi Moli, has complained to Sione — not through the formal channel — that a senior international staff member has been making demeaning comments about Kailani nationals and assigning lower-skilled tasks exclusively to the national staff. Tevi is afraid of retaliation. (C) Three team members have not received their skills development support (promised in their contracts) because Carlos reallocated the training budget to logistics. Maya finds this out when one team member mentions it during a routine check-in.

context_flag_effects.duty_of_care_upheld: Staff trust that leadership takes wellbeing seriously — they are more likely to report concerns early.
context_flag_effects.protection_framework_active: A misconduct reporting mechanism is already established — Tevi's complaint has a formal pathway.
context_flag_effects.no_flag_on_psea: No staff misconduct reporting mechanism exists — Tevi had nowhere to go except Sione.

scenes.status: placeholder — expand to ~150 words with Leila's messages, Tevi's fear, and the training budget discovery
scenes.image_prompt: PLACEHOLDER — Maya's phone screen showing a WhatsApp message thread from Leila: 'Day 3 blocked. I'm scared. No food for tonight.' On Maya's desk: a printed complaint note from Tevi, written by hand, referring to a senior international colleague's behaviour. Also on the desk: a budget reallocation form showing training funds moved to logistics. Flat illustration, desk in field office, multiple pressures visible simultaneously.

options.label: Keep Leila deployed; address staff issues informally; accept training budget reallocation
options.description_summary: Reassure Leila that the security concern is temporary. Ask Sione to speak informally with the senior international staff member. Accept Carlos's training budget reallocation as a necessary logistics trade-off.
requirements_missed: 8.4 — code of conduct violation not formally addressed
8.5 — Tevi's concern not handled through safe formal process
options.chs_learning_note: Informal handling of all three issues compounds the failures. Staff wellbeing, conduct standards, and professional development are all fundamental CHS 8 obligations.
options.consequence_summary: Leila and her team work three more weeks under increasing stress before one team member has a mental health crisis and returns to Nairobi. The senior international staff member continues the behaviour — two more national staff report it to Sione. The training budget reallocation sets a precedent.
options.delayed_impact: Leila files a formal duty of care complaint with HopeForward HQ. Three national staff turnover in a single month. An exit interview report cites 'leadership failure on staff wellbeing and discrimination' as the primary reason.

options.label: Evacuate Leila, formally address the discrimination complaint, restore training budget
options.description_summary: Formally suspend Northern Coast operations and evacuate Leila's team to the Talio hub. Open a formal HR investigation into the discrimination complaint (protecting Tevi from retaliation). Reverse the training budget reallocation — make a case to HQ for the cost of the commitment. Set up a confidential staff wellbeing check-in process.
options.chs_learning_note: Addressing all three staff management issues in parallel demonstrates that CHS 8 is lived at leadership level — not just policy on paper.
options.consequence_summary: Leila and her team reach Talio safely and thank Maya directly. The discrimination investigation is conducted confidentially and fairly. The senior international staff member receives a final written warning. Training budgets are restored. Staff retention in month 6 is the highest in HopeForward Kailani's history.
options.delayed_impact: Three national staff who had been considering leaving stay because of how leadership handled the discrimination case. Leila re-joins the programme in month 6 after a supported rest period.

options.label: Evacuate Leila; handle discrimination complaint informally; don't reverse training budget
options.description_summary: Evacuate Leila — her safety is non-negotiable. Speak privately to the senior international staff member but don't open a formal investigation. Accept the training reallocation as a done deal.
requirements_missed: 8.5 — Tevi's complaint not through formal channel
options.chs_learning_note: Partial action on duty of care is better than inaction, but informal handling of discrimination complaints leaves the discriminatory behaviour unaddressed and unrecorded.
options.consequence_summary: Leila is safe. But the senior international staff member, not formally disciplined, continues the behaviour with increased subtlety. Tevi submits a formal complaint to HQ directly two months later — bypassing Maya entirely because the informal approach didn't produce visible change.
options.delayed_impact: HQ launches a global HR audit of HopeForward Kailani after receiving Tevi's complaint. The informal handling of the original report is flagged as a management failure.

options.label: Restructure team management: wellbeing policy, staff forum, formal HR process for all three issues
options.description_summary: All of 8B, plus: use the moment to establish a permanent staff wellbeing policy — monthly check-ins, a confidential staff concerns hotline, a national staff advisory forum with real power. Document all three HR cases and their resolutions in HopeForward's policy review.
options.chs_learning_note: Beyond resolving individual incidents, CHS 8.1 and 8.7 call for systemic approaches — organisational culture and fair HR management. The three concurrent issues are also a diagnosis of system gaps.
options.consequence_summary: All three issues are resolved with appropriate processes and documented outcomes. The new national staff advisory forum surfaces two programme improvement ideas within a month. HopeForward Kailani has zero involuntary staff turnover in month 6.
options.delayed_impact: HopeForward global uses the Kailani staff wellbeing model in its updated HR guidance. Three other country programmes adopt the national staff advisory forum structure.

scenes.title: Three Failures, One Month
scenes.narrative_summary: Leila's team member breaks down in the field after a 14-hour checkpoint standoff and is medically evacuated. Tevi, after the informal conversation with his senior colleague 'changed nothing,' contacts HQ directly. Three national staff resign in the same week. Carlos, reviewing the month's metrics, notes that productivity dropped 30% in the Northern Coast. Exit interviews tell a consistent story: leadership chose operational metrics over people.
scenes.learning_point_summary: Staff are not resources to be managed around programme delivery — they are the programme. Every failure under CHS 8 produces a direct cost: in programme quality, in retention, and in the dignity of the people we employ.
scenes.chs_quote: Maintain a safe and inclusive working environment, taking measures to protect the safety, security and well-being and dignity of all staff and volunteers. (CHS 8.2)
scenes.image_prompt: PLACEHOLDER — Consequence scene for option 8A of Decision 8. Flat digital illustration, Pacific island humanitarian setting.

scenes.title: The Call That Mattered
scenes.narrative_summary: Leila arrives at Talio hub at 9pm. She calls Maya. 'Thank you for listening.' The discrimination investigation is hard and uncomfortable — a well-liked international staff member, evidence reviewed carefully, outcome communicated. Tevi receives a written update within two weeks. Three national staff who had started job searches decide to stay. By month 6, the team has the best retention rate in HopeForward's Asia-Pacific region.
scenes.learning_point_summary: Duty of care, code of conduct enforcement, and professional development support are not three separate obligations — they are three aspects of the same thing: treating your staff as fully human.
scenes.chs_quote: Take timely, appropriate actions to address misconduct of all staff and volunteers, in line with recognised good practice. (CHS 8.6)
scenes.image_prompt: PLACEHOLDER — Consequence scene for option 8B of Decision 8. Flat digital illustration, Pacific island humanitarian setting.

scenes.title: Safe But Not Protected
scenes.narrative_summary: Leila is in Talio and resting. Her team is relieved. But in the Port Hana office, the senior international staff member's behaviour continues — more carefully, but consistently. Tevi talks to two other national staff colleagues, who confirm they've experienced the same. Two months later, a formal complaint to HQ triggers the investigation that should have happened in month 5. The informal approach cost two months and significant staff distress.
scenes.learning_point_summary: Evacuating a team member from a security risk is essential. But leaving a discrimination complaint informally handled means the harm continues. Both are CHS 8 obligations.
scenes.chs_quote: Ensure all staff and volunteers understand and adhere to a code of conduct, which, at a minimum, prohibits any form of exploitation, abuse, harassment, or discrimination. (CHS 8.4)
scenes.image_prompt: PLACEHOLDER — Consequence scene for option 8C of Decision 8. Flat digital illustration, Pacific island humanitarian setting.

scenes.title: From Crisis to Culture
scenes.narrative_summary: The three concurrent issues become the foundation for something different. The national staff advisory forum meets for the first time and immediately identifies three programme improvements that international staff hadn't seen. The wellbeing check-ins reveal that two other staff members were struggling silently. The discrimination case is resolved fairly and sets a precedent that is widely known and respected across the team.
